Cysteine has a thiol group (pKa ~8.3) that is mostly protonated and neutral at pH 7.4. Neither carries a negative charge under physiological conditions.

D. Asparagine and Glutamine: Asparagine and glutamine are the amide derivatives of aspartate and glutamate, respectively. Their side chains contain -CONH2 groups, which are uncharged and polar. They are easily confused with their acidic counterparts because of similar names, but the -NH2 on the amide prevents ionization at physiological pH.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: Aspartate and glutamate are the two acidic (negatively charged) amino acids; lysine, arginine, and histidine are the basic (positively charged) ones.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid is the pH at which:
options: The amino acid carries a net positive charge
The amino acid carries a net negative charge
The amino acid has no net electrical charge
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: The amino acid has no net electrical charge

The isoelectric point (pI) is the pH at which an amino acid (or protein) exists as a zwitterion with a net charge of zero. At this pH, the molecule does not migrate in an electric field.

Importantly, "no net charge" does not mean "no charges" — the molecule still has both positive and negative charges (it is a zwitterion), but they are balanced. For a simple amino acid with no ionizable side chain, pI = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐ₂) / 2, where pKₐ₁ is for –COOH and pKₐ₂ is for –NH₃⁺.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. The amino acid carries a net positive charge: An amino acid carries a net positive charge when the pH is below its isoelectric point. At low pH, the amino group is protonated (-NH3+) and the carboxyl group may also remain protonated (-COOH), giving an overall positive charge. This is the opposite of the pI, where net charge is zero.

B. The amino acid carries a net negative charge: An amino acid carries a net negative charge when the pH is above its isoelectric point. At high pH, the carboxyl group is deprotonated (-COO-) and the amino group loses its proton (-NH2), resulting in an overall negative charge. At the pI itself, positive and negative charges are balanced.

D. All functional groups are uncharged: At the isoelectric point, the functional groups are NOT all uncharged. Instead, they carry equal and opposite charges that cancel out. The amino group is protonated (-NH3+) and the carboxyl group is deprotonated (-COO-), creating a zwitterion with zero net charge but with both charged groups present.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: The isoelectric point (pI) is the pH where the net charge is zero. The molecule is still a zwitterion with balanced positive and negative charges.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which type of bond directly links amino acids in the primary structure of a protein?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Peptide bond

The primary structure of a protein is defined as the linear sequence of amino acids, connected by peptide bonds. The peptide bond is a covalent amide linkage (–CO–NH–) formed between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of the next.

Hydrogen bonds stabilize secondary structure. Ionic bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and van der Waals forces contribute to tertiary structure. Disulfide bonds are covalent but link cysteine side chains to stabilize tertiary/quaternary structure — they are not part of the primary backbone sequence.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Hydrogen bond: Hydrogen bonds stabilize secondary structure.

B. Ionic bond: Ionic bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and van der waals forces contribute to tertiary structure.

D. Disulfide bond: Disulfide bonds are covalent but link cysteine side chains to stabilize tertiary/quaternary structure — they are not part of the primary backbone sequence.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: Primary protein structure = amino acid sequence connected by peptide (amide) bonds along the polypeptide backbone.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: In L-amino acids, the amino group on the α-carbon is oriented to the:
options: Above the α-carbon in a Fischer projection
Below the α-carbon in a Fischer projection
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Left in a Fischer projection

By convention in a Fischer projection, the carboxyl group is placed at the top and the R group at the bottom. In L-amino acids, the amino group (–NH₂) is on the left side of the α-carbon. In D-amino acids, the amino group is on the right side.

Nearly all amino acids found in proteins are in the L-configuration. The naming follows the same convention used for L- and D-glyceraldehyde. The L/D designation refers to the absolute configuration and is unrelated to the direction of optical rotation (+/−).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Right in a Fischer projection: If the amino group is on the right side of the α-carbon in a Fischer projection, the amino acid is designated as D (from Latin dexter, meaning right). D-amino acids are rare in nature but are found in some bacterial cell walls and certain marine organisms. Nearly all amino acids in proteins are L-form.

C. Above the α-carbon in a Fischer projection: In a Fischer projection of an amino acid, the groups above and below the α-carbon are the amino group (top) or carboxyl group (top, by convention) and the R group (bottom). The L/D designation is specifically about whether the amino group projects to the left or right, not about vertical positioning.

D. Below the α-carbon in a Fischer projection: The vertical positions in a Fischer projection of an amino acid are conventionally occupied by the carboxyl group (top) and the R group or hydrogen (bottom). The horizontal positions determine L vs D configuration. The amino group projects to the left for L-amino acids.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: In Fischer projections, L-amino acids have the amino group on the left. Virtually all biological proteins consist of L-amino acids.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: An α-helix makes one complete turn every:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: 3.6 amino acid residues

The α-helix is a right-handed helical structure in which the polypeptide backbone coils around a central axis. Each complete turn contains 3.6 amino acid residues and has a pitch (rise per turn) of 0.54 nm. Each residue contributes a rise of 0.15 nm along the helix axis.

The helix is stabilized by hydrogen bonds between the C=O group of residue i and the N–H group of residue i+4. The side chains project outward from the helix backbone. Proline and glycine tend to disrupt α-helices — proline due to its rigid ring and glycine due to its excessive conformational flexibility.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 2.5 amino acid residues: 2.5 residues per turn does not correspond to any common protein secondary structure. The α-helix has 3.6 residues per turn with a pitch (rise per turn) of 5.4 angstroms. This specific geometry allows each backbone N-H to hydrogen bond with the C=O of the amino acid four residues earlier (i to i-4 pattern).

C. 5.0 amino acid residues: 5.0 residues per turn does not match the α-helix. Some specialized helices exist, such as the pi-helix (4.4 residues per turn) and the 310-helix (3.0 residues per turn), but the standard α-helix discovered by Pauling and Corey has exactly 3.6 residues per turn.

D. 7.2 amino acid residues: 7.2 residues per turn is exactly double the correct value (3.6 x 2) but does not correspond to any known protein helix. The 3.6-residue periodicity is a fundamental parameter worth memorizing, as it determines which residues are on the same face of the helix.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: The α-helix has 3.6 residues per turn, stabilized by i → i+4 hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which amino acid is the only one that contains a secondary amino group in its backbone?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Proline

Proline is the only standard amino acid that has a secondary amino group (an imino group) in its backbone. Its cyclic pyrrolidine side chain connects to both the α-carbon and the backbone nitrogen, creating a secondary amine. All other standard amino acids have primary amino groups (–NH₂) in their free form.

This structural feature has important consequences: the peptide bond preceding proline can adopt a cis configuration more readily than other peptide bonds, and the N atom in proline cannot donate a hydrogen bond when it is part of a peptide chain (it has no N–H). This is why proline disrupts regular secondary structures.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Glycine: Glycine is unique for having the simplest side chain (just a hydrogen atom), making it the smallest amino acid with no chirality at the α-carbon. However, glycine has a standard primary amino group (-NH2) on its backbone, not a secondary one.

C. Tryptophan: Tryptophan contains a large aromatic indole ring system and is the largest of the standard amino acids. It has a primary amino group on the backbone like most amino acids. Its indole nitrogen is part of the aromatic ring, not the backbone amine.

D. Histidine: Histidine contains an imidazole ring in its side chain, which has important acid-base catalytic properties (pKa ~6.0). While the imidazole ring contains nitrogen atoms, the backbone amino group of histidine is a standard primary amine. Only proline has the cyclic side chain that connects back to the backbone nitrogen.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: Proline's cyclic structure creates a secondary (imino) nitrogen in the backbone, restricting rotation and affecting protein folding.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Collagen's structure is characterized by a repeating tripeptide motif. Which amino acid frequently occupies every third position?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Glycine

Collagen has a unique triple-helical structure composed of three polypeptide chains wound around each other. The repeating motif is Gly–X–Y, where Glycine appears at every third position. X is frequently proline and Y is often hydroxyproline.

Glycine must occupy every third position because it is the smallest amino acid (R = H). At the center of the triple helix, there is only enough space for hydrogen atoms. Any larger side chain would cause steric clashes and destabilize the structure. Mutations replacing glycine in collagen cause diseases like osteogenesis imperfecta (brittle bone disease).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Alanine: Alanine is a small, nonpolar amino acid but is not the one required at every third position in collagen. The Gly-X-Y repeating motif specifically requires glycine because the interior of the collagen triple helix is so tightly packed that only glycine (with its single hydrogen atom side chain) can fit.

B. Proline: X is frequently proline and y is often hydroxyproline.

D. Leucine: Leucine is a large, branched, hydrophobic amino acid that would be far too bulky to fit into the crowded interior of the collagen triple helix. The three polypeptide chains wind around each other so tightly that every third residue must be glycine, the smallest amino acid, to avoid steric clashes.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: Collagen's Gly–X–Y repeat requires glycine at every third position due to spatial constraints in the triple helix interior.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: An enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of peptide bonds is classified as a:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Hydrolase (protease)

Enzymes that cleave peptide bonds using water are classified as hydrolases, specifically proteases (also called peptidases or proteinases). Hydrolases (EC class 3) catalyze bond cleavage by the addition of water.

Enzyme ClassFunctionExample
Oxidoreductases (EC 1)Redox reactionsLactate dehydrogenase
Transferases (EC 2)Group transferKinases
Hydrolases (EC 3)HydrolysisProteases, lipases
Lyases (EC 4)Non-hydrolytic cleavageDecarboxylases
Isomerases (EC 5)IsomerizationPhosphoglucose isomerase
Ligases (EC 6)Bond formation with ATPDNA ligase

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Ligase: Its primary function is bond formation with atp, which differs from what the question asks about.

B. Transferase: Its primary function is group transfer, which differs from what the question asks about.

D. Lyase: Its primary function is non-hydrolytic cleavage, which differs from what the question asks about.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

Bottom Line: Proteases are hydrolases that cleave peptide bonds by adding water across the bond.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the IUPAC name of the compound CH₃CH(CH₃)CH₂CH₃?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: A. 2-methylbutane

The longest carbon chain has 4 carbons (butane). A methyl group (CH₃) is attached to carbon 2. Numbering from the end nearest the branch gives the substituent the lowest locant, yielding 2-methylbutane.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. 3-methylbutane: "3-methylbutane" violates the IUPAC rule that numbering must start from the end nearest to the branch point. The longest chain is butane (4 carbons), and the methyl branch is on carbon 2 (not carbon 3). Always number from the end that gives the lowest locant to the substituent.

C. 2-methylpentane: 2-methylpentane would have a total of 6 carbons (5 in the main chain + 1 methyl branch). The given formula CH3CH(CH3)CH2CH3 has only 5 carbons total, so the longest chain is 4 carbons (butane), not 5 (pentane). Counting carbons carefully is essential for correct IUPAC naming.

D. isopentane: "Isopentane" is a common (trivial) name, not the IUPAC systematic name. While isopentane does refer to the same compound (2-methylbutane), IUPAC nomenclature is the preferred system in chemistry because it unambiguously describes the structure.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which reagent is used to distinguish between ethene and ethane?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Bromine water

Bromine water (Br₂/H₂O) is decolorized by ethene (CH₂=CH₂) through electrophilic addition across the double bond, but does not react with ethane under normal conditions. This is a standard test for unsaturation.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Dilute NaOH: Dilute NaOH is a weak base that does not react with either ethene or ethane under normal conditions. It is used for neutralizing acids or hydrolyzing esters, not for testing unsaturation. Neither alkanes nor simple alkenes react with aqueous NaOH.

C. Concentrated H₂SO₄: Concentrated H2SO4 can react with alkenes (addition across the double bond to form alkyl hydrogen sulfates), but it is not a clean diagnostic test because it can also react with other functional groups. Bromine water is preferred because its visible color change (orange to colorless) is specific and unambiguous for unsaturation.

D. Anhydrous AlCl₃: Anhydrous AlCl3 is a Lewis acid catalyst used in Friedel-Crafts reactions with aromatic compounds, not a reagent for testing unsaturation in simple alkenes. It does not selectively distinguish between alkanes and alkenes in a simple bench test.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: According to Markovnikov's rule, the addition of HBr to propene produces mainly:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. 2-bromopropane

Markovnikov's rule states that the hydrogen atom adds to the carbon of the double bond bearing more hydrogen atoms, while the halide adds to the more substituted carbon. In propene (CH₃CH=CH₂), the more stable secondary carbocation intermediate leads to 2-bromopropane as the major product.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 1-bromopropane: 1-bromopropane would be the anti-Markovnikov product, where bromine adds to the less substituted carbon. This occurs in peroxide-initiated (radical) addition of HBr. Under normal (ionic) conditions, Markovnikov's rule applies: the hydrogen adds to the carbon with more hydrogens, and the halide adds to the more substituted carbon.

C. 1,2-dibromopropane: 1,2-dibromopropane would result from the addition of Br2 (molecular bromine) across the double bond, not HBr. Adding Br2 to propene gives both bromine atoms adding across the double bond in an anti-addition fashion, which is a different reaction entirely from hydrohalogenation.

D. 3-bromopropane: "3-bromopropane" is the same as 1-bromopropane (just numbered from the other end of the chain). In either case, this would be the anti-Markovnikov product, not the major product under standard ionic addition conditions.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The hybridization of carbon atoms in ethyne (C₂H₂) is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. sp

In ethyne, each carbon forms one triple bond (one σ and two π bonds) and one single bond to hydrogen. This requires two hybrid orbitals, corresponding to sp hybridization. The bond angle is 180°, giving ethyne a linear geometry.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. sp³: sp3 hybridization gives a tetrahedral geometry with four σ bonds and no π bonds. In ethyne, each carbon forms only two σ bonds (one C-H and one C-C) and two π bonds, requiring only two hybrid orbitals. The two unhybridized p orbitals form the two π bonds of the triple bond.

B. sp²: sp2 hybridization gives a trigonal planar geometry with three σ bonds and one π bond, as seen in ethene (C2H4). Ethyne has a triple bond requiring two π bonds, so carbon needs two unhybridized p orbitals. Only sp hybridization (two hybrid orbitals + two unhybridized p orbitals) satisfies this.

D. sp³d: sp3d hybridization requires d-orbital involvement and creates five bonding regions (trigonal bipyramidal). Carbon atoms in Period 2 do not have accessible d orbitals and cannot undergo sp3d hybridization. This type of hybridization is seen in elements like phosphorus in PCl5.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following alkynes gives acetone (propan-2-one) upon acid-catalyzed hydration?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Propyne

Terminal alkynes like propyne (CH₃C≡CH) undergo keto-enol tautomerism when hydrated in the presence of acid and Hg²⁺ catalyst. Propyne gives acetone (propan-2-one) following Markovnikov addition of water. But-2-yne is an internal alkyne and would give a different product pattern.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Ethyne: Ethyne (HC triple bond CH) is a terminal alkyne, but it has no adjacent carbon with hydrogens to form a methyl ketone via tautomerism. When ethyne undergoes acid-catalyzed hydration, it forms acetaldehyde (an aldehyde, CH3CHO), not a ketone, because it has only two carbons.

C. But-1-yne: But-1-yne (CH3CH2C triple bond CH) is also a terminal alkyne, but acid-catalyzed hydration produces 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone), not acetone. Only propyne yields acetone. However, the question focuses on keto-enol tautomerism, and propyne is the classic textbook example that yields acetone, the simplest ketone, through this process.

D. But-2-yne: But-2-yne is an internal alkyne and would give a different product pattern.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the product when 2-butene reacts with ozone (O₃) followed by reductive workup with Zn/H₂O?
options: 1 molecule of formaldehyde + 1 molecule of propanal
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: A. 2 molecules of acetaldehyde

Ozonolysis cleaves the double bond. 2-Butene (CH₃CH=CHCH₃) has the double bond between C2 and C3. Cleavage produces two fragments: CH₃CHO (acetaldehyde) from each side. Reductive workup with Zn prevents further oxidation to carboxylic acids.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. 1 molecule of formaldehyde + 1 molecule of propanal: 1 molecule of formaldehyde + 1 molecule of propanal would result from ozonolysis of 1-butene (CH2=CHCH2CH3), where the double bond is between C1 and C2. In 2-butene, the double bond is symmetrically placed between C2 and C3, so the cleavage produces two identical fragments (CH3CHO, acetaldehyde).

C. 2 molecules of formaldehyde: 2 molecules of formaldehyde would result from ozonolysis of ethene (CH2=CH2), where each carbon of the double bond has two hydrogens. For 2-butene (CH3CH=CHCH3), each carbon of the double bond bears a methyl group, yielding acetaldehyde (CH3CHO) rather than formaldehyde (HCHO).

D. 1 molecule of butanal: 1 molecule of butanal would imply that the molecule was not cleaved at the double bond, which contradicts the mechanism of ozonolysis. Ozonolysis always breaks the carbon-carbon double bond, producing two separate carbonyl fragments. The carbon count must add up: two C2 aldehydes (acetaldehyde), not one C4 aldehyde.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The free-radical chlorination of methane proceeds via which steps in order?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: A. Initiation → Propagation → Termination

Free-radical halogenation follows three steps: (1) Initiation — UV light or heat causes homolytic cleavage of Cl₂ to form Cl· radicals. (2) Propagation — chain-carrying steps where Cl· abstracts H from CH₄ forming ·CH₃, which reacts with Cl₂. (3) Termination — two radicals combine, ending the chain.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. Propagation → Initiation → Termination: Propagation cannot come before initiation. Initiation is the essential first step where UV light or heat breaks the Cl-Cl bond homolytically to generate chlorine radicals. Without these initial radicals, the chain-carrying propagation steps (Cl radical abstracting H from methane, then CH3 radical reacting with Cl2) cannot begin.

C. Termination → Propagation → Initiation: Termination cannot be the first step because termination occurs when two radicals combine to form a stable molecule (e.g., Cl + Cl, CH3 + CH3, or CH3 + Cl). There are no radicals present at the start of the reaction, so termination events cannot occur until after initiation and propagation have generated them.

D. Initiation → Termination → Propagation: Termination cannot immediately follow initiation. After the initial radicals are generated in initiation, the propagation cycle must run (often thousands of times) to produce the chlorinated product. Termination eventually ends the chain when radicals happen to collide with each other.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Cyclopropane is more reactive than cyclohexane because:
options: Its C–C bonds have significant angle strain
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Its C–C bonds have significant angle strain

In cyclopropane, the internal bond angles are forced to 60°, far from the ideal tetrahedral angle of 109.5° for sp³ carbons. This angle strain (Baeyer strain) makes the C–C bonds weaker and the molecule more reactive. Cyclohexane adopts a chair conformation with nearly ideal bond angles and minimal strain.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. It has fewer hydrogen atoms: Having fewer hydrogen atoms does not make a cycloalkane more reactive. Cyclopropane has 6 hydrogens (C3H6) compared to cyclohexane's 12 (C6H12), but the difference in reactivity comes from ring strain, not hydrogen count. The 60-degree bond angles in cyclopropane deviate enormously from the ideal sp3 angle of 109.5 degrees.

B. It has more π electrons: Cyclopropane has no pi electrons; it contains only σ bonds. Pi electrons are found in unsaturated compounds like alkenes and aromatic rings. Cyclopropane's high reactivity stems from the severe angle strain forcing its C-C bonds to bend, creating "banana bonds" that store extra energy and make the ring eager to open.

D. It is aromatic: Cyclopropane is definitely not aromatic. Aromaticity requires a planar, cyclic, fully conjugated system of pi electrons satisfying Huckel's rule (4n+2 pi electrons). Cyclopropane has no pi electrons and no conjugation. Its reactivity is entirely due to angle strain, not aromatic character.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following has the highest boiling point?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: D. Cyclopentane

Among C₅ hydrocarbons, increased branching leads to a more spherical shape and lower surface area, reducing van der Waals forces and lowering the boiling point. Pentane (36°C) > 2-methylbutane (28°C) > 2,2-dimethylpropane (10°C). Cyclopentane (49°C) has a rigid cyclic structure with even greater surface contact area, giving it the highest boiling point of the group.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Pentane: Pentane (36°c) > 2-methylbutane (28°c) > 2,2-dimethylpropane (10°c).

B. 2-methylbutane: Pentane (36°c) > 2-methylbutane (28°c) > 2,2-dimethylpropane (10°c).

C. 2,2-dimethylpropane: Pentane (36°c) > 2-methylbutane (28°c) > 2,2-dimethylpropane (10°c).

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Treatment of propyne with sodium amide (NaNH₂) in liquid ammonia followed by methyl iodide (CH₃I) gives:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. But-2-yne

NaNH₂ is a strong enough base to deprotonate the terminal alkyne hydrogen of propyne (CH₃C≡CH), forming the acetylide anion CH₃C≡C−Na⁺. This nucleophile then undergoes SN2 reaction with CH₃I to give CH₃C≡CCH₃ (but-2-yne).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Butane: Butane (C4H10) is a fully saturated alkane with no triple bond. The reaction of a terminal alkyne with NaNH2 creates an acetylide anion (a carbon nucleophile), which then attacks methyl iodide in an SN2 reaction. This preserves the triple bond, so the product must be an alkyne, not an alkane.

B. But-1-yne: But-1-yne (CH3CH2C triple bond CH) would require adding an ethyl group to acetylene, not a methyl group to propyne. Starting from propyne (CH3C triple bond CH), the NaNH2 removes the terminal H to give CH3C triple bond C- Na+, which reacts with CH3I to give CH3C triple bond C-CH3 (but-2-yne), an internal alkyne.

D. Pent-2-yne: Pent-2-yne (CH3C triple bond CCH2CH3) has five carbons. Since propyne has 3 carbons and methyl iodide adds 1 carbon, the product can only have 4 carbons total. Carbon count is a quick way to eliminate wrong answers in synthesis problems.

Key Educational Points:

  • Organic chemistry centers on carbon's ability to form four stable bonds, creating chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited complexity.
  • Functional groups determine chemical reactivity: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -NH2 (amine), C=O (carbonyl), etc.
  • Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: According to Hückel's rule, a planar cyclic compound is aromatic if it has:
options: Any even number of π electrons
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. (4n + 2) π electrons

Hückel's rule states that a planar, cyclic, fully conjugated molecule is aromatic if it contains (4n + 2) π electrons, where n is a non-negative integer. For benzene, n = 1, giving 6 π electrons. Compounds with 4n π electrons are anti-aromatic.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 4n π electrons: Compounds with 4n π electrons are anti-aromatic.

C. 4n + 1 π electrons: 4n + 1 is an odd number of pi electrons, which does not correspond to any standard aromaticity rule. Aromatic stability requires an even number of pi electrons following the (4n + 2) formula, where n = 0, 1, 2, 3... giving 2, 6, 10, 14 pi electrons. Benzene has 6 (n=1), naphthalene has 10 (n=2).

D. Any even number of π electrons: Simply having an even number of pi electrons does not guarantee aromaticity. For example, cyclooctatetraene has 8 pi electrons (an even number), but 8 does not fit the (4n + 2) formula. Instead, 8 = 4(2), making it anti-aromatic if planar. Cyclooctatetraene avoids this by adopting a non-planar tub shape.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which substituent is an ortho/para director in electrophilic aromatic substitution?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. -OH

The hydroxyl group (-OH) is an activating group that donates electron density to the ring through resonance (lone pairs on oxygen). This increases electron density at the ortho and para positions, directing incoming electrophiles there. -NO₂, -COOH, and -CHO are all electron-withdrawing and are meta directors.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. -NO₂: The nitro group (-NO2) is a strong electron-withdrawing group and a meta director. It pulls electron density away from the ring through both resonance and induction, deactivating the ring and directing incoming electrophiles to the meta position. All strong electron-withdrawing groups are meta directors and ring deactivators.

B. -COOH: -no2, -cooh, and -cho are all electron-withdrawing and are meta directors.

D. -CHO: -no2, -cooh, and -cho are all electron-withdrawing and are meta directors.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Nitration of benzene uses a mixture of concentrated HNO₃ and H₂SO₄. The active electrophile is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. NO₂⁺

Concentrated sulfuric acid protonates nitric acid, which then loses water to generate the nitronium ion (NO₂⁺). This powerful electrophile attacks the electron-rich benzene ring in the rate-determining step of electrophilic aromatic substitution.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. NO₂⁻: NO2- (nitrite ion) is a nucleophile, not an electrophile. Electrophilic aromatic substitution requires an electron-poor species that attacks the electron-rich benzene ring. The nitronium ion (NO2+) is generated when concentrated H2SO4 protonates HNO3, causing it to lose water and form the powerful electrophile.

B. NO₃⁻: NO3- (nitrate ion) is a stable, negatively charged species that acts as a nucleophile, not an electrophile. It is the conjugate base of nitric acid. In the nitration mechanism, H2SO4 protonates HNO3 to generate H2NO3+, which then loses water to form the active electrophile NO2+ (nitronium ion).

D. NO⁺: NO+ (nitrosonium ion) is an electrophile but is involved in nitrosation reactions, not nitration. Nitrosation typically occurs with nitrous acid (HNO2) and is important in diazonium salt formation from amines. The nitration of benzene specifically uses the nitronium ion NO2+, which adds -NO2 to the ring.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Toluene undergoes electrophilic bromination faster than benzene because the methyl group is:
options: An electron-donating group by hyperconjugation and induction
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. An electron-donating group by hyperconjugation and induction

The methyl group donates electron density to the benzene ring through both positive inductive effect (+I) and hyperconjugation. This increases the electron density of the ring, making it more nucleophilic and thus more reactive toward electrophiles. The methyl group is an activating, ortho/para-directing substituent.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. An electron-withdrawing group by induction: The methyl group is actually an electron-donating group, not electron-withdrawing. While alkyl groups do have a very slight inductive withdrawal due to the sp3 carbon being somewhat more electronegative than sp2, this effect is overwhelmed by hyperconjugation, where the C-H σ bond electrons stabilize the ring. The net result is electron donation to the ring.

C. A meta director: A meta director directs incoming electrophiles preferentially to the meta position. Electron-withdrawing groups like -NO2, -CN, and -COOH are meta directors. The methyl group, being electron-donating, is an ortho/para director. It activates the ortho and para positions by increasing electron density at those sites through hyperconjugation.

D. A deactivating substituent: A deactivating substituent slows down electrophilic aromatic substitution by withdrawing electron density from the ring. The methyl group is an activating substituent because it donates electrons to the ring, making toluene react faster than benzene. All electron-donating groups are activating and ortho/para directing.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Friedel-Crafts alkylation of benzene with CH₃Cl in the presence of AlCl₃ produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Toluene

In Friedel-Crafts alkylation, AlCl₃ acts as a Lewis acid catalyst, generating the electrophilic carbocation CH₃⁺ (or a strongly polarized complex) from CH₃Cl. This electrophile attacks benzene to give methylbenzene (toluene) plus HCl.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Chlorobenzene: Chlorobenzene would be produced by electrophilic aromatic halogenation (Cl2/AlCl3 or FeCl3), where chlorine itself substitutes onto the ring. In Friedel-Crafts alkylation, AlCl3 generates a carbocation (CH3+) from CH3Cl, and this methyl cation is the electrophile that attacks the ring, giving toluene (methylbenzene).

C. Benzaldehyde: Benzaldehyde (C6H5CHO) would be produced by Friedel-Crafts acylation using formyl chloride (HCOCl) or by other specialized methods. Using CH3Cl as the alkylating agent can only introduce a methyl group, not an aldehyde functional group.

D. Phenol: Phenol (C6H5OH) has a hydroxyl group bonded to the benzene ring and is not a product of Friedel-Crafts reactions. Phenol is typically prepared by the cumene process industrially, or by nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Friedel-Crafts alkylation strictly introduces alkyl groups onto the ring.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which compound is NOT aromatic?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Cyclooctatetraene

Cyclooctatetraene (COT) has 8 π electrons (4n where n = 2), which makes it anti-aromatic if planar. To avoid anti-aromaticity, COT adopts a non-planar tub-shaped conformation and behaves as a non-aromatic polyene. Pyridine, furan, and thiophene are all aromatic heterocycles with 6 π electrons.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Pyridine: Pyridine, furan, and thiophene are all aromatic heterocycles with 6 π electrons.

C. Furan: Pyridine, furan, and thiophene are all aromatic heterocycles with 6 π electrons.

D. Thiophene: Pyridine, furan, and thiophene are all aromatic heterocycles with 6 π electrons.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: When chlorobenzene undergoes further nitration, the major product is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. o- and p-nitrochlorobenzene

Chlorine is an ortho/para director despite being a ring deactivator. Although Cl withdraws electron density through its inductive effect (-I), it donates electron density through resonance (+M) from its lone pairs. The resonance effect selectively stabilizes the intermediate arenium ion at ortho and para positions.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. m-nitrochlorobenzene: m-nitrochlorobenzene would be the major product if chlorine were a meta director. However, chlorine is an ortho/para director (despite being a ring deactivator). Chlorine has lone pairs that donate electron density to the ring by resonance, favoring ortho and para positions, even though its inductive effect is electron-withdrawing.

C. 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene: 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene requires two nitration steps and would only form as a minor product under forcing conditions (excess HNO3/H2SO4, high temperature). The first nitration gives predominantly ortho and para products, and a second nitration would require harsher conditions because the -NO2 group strongly deactivates the ring.

D. m-chloronitrobenzene: m-chloronitrobenzene is actually the same compound as m-nitrochlorobenzene (just named differently). Since chlorine is an ortho/para director, the meta product is only a minor byproduct. The naming convention depends on which substituent is considered the parent, but the meta isomer is not the major product either way.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons has 10 π electrons?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Naphthalene

Naphthalene (C₁₀H₈) consists of two fused benzene rings sharing an edge. It has 10 π electrons (4n + 2, where n = 2), satisfying Hückel's rule. Benzene has 6, while anthracene and phenanthrene each have 14 π electrons.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Benzene: Benzene has 6, while anthracene and phenanthrene each have 14 π electrons.

C. Anthracene: Benzene has 6, while anthracene and phenanthrene each have 14 π electrons.

D. Phenanthrene: Benzene has 6, while anthracene and phenanthrene each have 14 π electrons.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Phenol is more acidic than ethanol because:
options: Phenol has a higher molecular weight
The phenoxide ion is stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring
The O–H bond in phenol is shorter
Ethanol has a stronger inductive effect
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. The phenoxide ion is stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring

When phenol loses a proton, the resulting phenoxide ion (C₆H₅O−) delocalizes the negative charge into the aromatic ring through resonance, stabilizing the conjugate base. Ethoxide cannot do this, so ethanol is a much weaker acid (pKₐ ≈ 16 vs. phenol pKₐ ≈ 10).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Phenol has a higher molecular weight: Molecular weight has no direct relationship to acidity. Acidity depends on how well the conjugate base is stabilized after losing a proton. Phenol (MW 94) is more acidic than ethanol (MW 46) not because it is heavier, but because the phenoxide ion (C6H5O-) is resonance-stabilized by the benzene ring.

C. The O–H bond in phenol is shorter: Bond length differences between O-H bonds in phenol and ethanol are negligible and not the reason for the acidity difference. The key factor is the stability of the conjugate base. In phenoxide, the negative charge is delocalized across the aromatic ring through resonance, greatly stabilizing the anion compared to ethoxide.

D. Ethanol has a stronger inductive effect: Ethanol does not have a "stronger inductive effect" that would explain the acidity difference. In fact, the ethyl group in ethanol is electron-donating, which destabilizes the ethoxide anion by pushing electron density toward the already negatively charged oxygen. In phenol, the aromatic ring withdraws and delocalizes the negative charge.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Benzene reacts with acetyl chloride (CH₃COCl) in the presence of AlCl₃ to give:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Acetophenone

This is a Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction. AlCl₃ generates the acylium ion CH₃CO⁺ from acetyl chloride, which attacks benzene to give acetophenone (C₆H₅COCH₃). Unlike alkylation, acylation does not suffer from polysubstitution because the product ketone is deactivated.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Toluene: Toluene (methylbenzene) is the product of Friedel-Crafts alkylation with CH3Cl/AlCl3, not acylation. Friedel-Crafts acylation uses an acyl chloride (RCOCl) and introduces a C=O-containing group (acyl group) onto the ring. Acetyl chloride (CH3COCl) gives acetophenone (C6H5COCH3).

C. Benzaldehyde: Benzaldehyde (C6H5CHO) would require formyl chloride (HCOCl) as the acylating agent, not acetyl chloride. Since the reagent is CH3COCl (acetyl chloride), the product has a -COCH3 group on the ring, which is acetophenone. Benzaldehyde has a -CHO group instead.

D. Benzoic acid: Benzoic acid (C6H5COOH) is a carboxylic acid that is not directly produced by Friedel-Crafts acylation. It can be made by oxidizing toluene with KMnO4. Friedel-Crafts acylation gives a ketone (acetophenone), and an advantage of acylation over alkylation is that the ketone product does not rearrange or undergo multiple substitutions.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which type of alcohol is most easily oxidized to a carboxylic acid?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Primary alcohol

Primary alcohols (RCH₂OH) can be oxidized first to an aldehyde (RCHO) and then further to a carboxylic acid (RCOOH) using strong oxidizing agents like KMnO₄ or K₂Cr₂O₇. Secondary alcohols oxidize to ketones only. Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation under normal conditions because they lack an H on the carbon bearing the -OH.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Tertiary alcohol: Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation under normal conditions because they lack an h on the carbon bearing the -oh.

B. Secondary alcohol: Secondary alcohols oxidize to ketones only.

D. Phenol: Phenol has the -OH group bonded directly to an aromatic ring, which makes its oxidation chemistry completely different from aliphatic alcohols. Phenol does not undergo simple oxidation to a carboxylic acid like primary alcohols do. Instead, phenol can be oxidized to quinones under certain conditions, which is a fundamentally different pathway.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The Lucas test distinguishes between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols. A tertiary alcohol reacts with Lucas reagent (ZnCl₂/conc. HCl):
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Immediately at room temperature

Tertiary alcohols form stable carbocations rapidly (SN1 mechanism), so they react immediately with Lucas reagent to form an alkyl chloride, visible as a cloudy layer. Secondary alcohols react within 5 minutes, while primary alcohols require prolonged heating or do not react at room temperature.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Only upon heating: "Only upon heating" describes the behavior of a primary alcohol with Lucas reagent (ZnCl2/conc. HCl). Primary alcohols react very slowly because their carbocation intermediates (primary carbocations) are highly unstable. Heating provides the activation energy needed for this sluggish reaction.

B. Within 5 minutes at room temperature: "Within 5 minutes at room temperature" describes the behavior of a secondary alcohol with Lucas reagent. Secondary carbocations are more stable than primary ones, so the reaction proceeds at room temperature but still takes a few minutes. The cloudiness (from insoluble alkyl chloride) appears gradually.

D. Not at all: Tertiary alcohols absolutely do react with Lucas reagent, and they do so the fastest of all. The tertiary carbocation (R3C+) formed is the most stable due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects from three alkyl groups. This is why the turbidity appears immediately, making the Lucas test a convenient way to classify alcohols.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Dehydration of 2-methylbutan-2-ol with concentrated H₂SO₄ gives primarily:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. 2-methylbut-2-ene

According to Zaitsev's rule, the major product of elimination is the more substituted alkene. Dehydration of a tertiary alcohol proceeds via E1 mechanism through a stable tertiary carbocation. Removal of a proton from C3 gives the trisubstituted 2-methylbut-2-ene as the major product over the less substituted 2-methylbut-1-ene.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 2-methylbut-1-ene: 2-methylbut-1-ene is the less substituted alkene (Hofmann product). According to Zaitsev's rule, the major product of E1 elimination (favored for tertiary alcohols with acid catalyst) is the more substituted alkene. 2-methylbut-2-ene (trisubstituted) is more stable than 2-methylbut-1-ene (disubstituted) and is therefore the major product.

C. 3-methylbut-1-ene: 3-methylbut-1-ene is not a feasible product from this dehydration. Eliminating water from 2-methylbutan-2-ol involves removing the -OH from C2 and a proton from an adjacent carbon (C1 or C3). This cannot produce 3-methylbut-1-ene because that would require a structural rearrangement of the carbon skeleton.

D. Pentene: "Pentene" is too vague an answer and does not correctly describe the product. The dehydration preserves the methyl branch, so the product is a branched alkene (2-methylbut-2-ene), not a straight-chain pentene. Always consider the full structural formula when predicting elimination products.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which reagent selectively oxidizes a primary alcohol to an aldehyde without further oxidation?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate)

PCC is a mild oxidizing agent that converts primary alcohols to aldehydes without further oxidation to carboxylic acids. It works in anhydrous conditions (typically in CH₂Cl₂). Strong oxidants like KMnO₄ and K₂Cr₂O₇ would push the oxidation to the carboxylic acid stage.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. KMnO₄ (acidic): KMnO4 in acidic conditions is a strong oxidizing agent that will oxidize a primary alcohol all the way to a carboxylic acid (R-CH2OH to R-COOH). It cannot stop at the aldehyde stage. KMnO4 is also used to cleave double bonds (ozonolysis alternative) and is the reagent in the Baeyer test for unsaturation.

B. K₂Cr₂O₇/H⁺: K2Cr2O7/H+ (acidified potassium dichromate) is another strong oxidant that converts primary alcohols to carboxylic acids, not aldehydes. The aqueous acidic conditions facilitate further oxidation of the intermediate aldehyde. PCC works because it operates in anhydrous conditions (dichloromethane solvent), preventing overoxidation.

D. Concentrated HNO₃: Concentrated HNO3 is a strong oxidizing acid, but it is not typically used for selective alcohol oxidation. It tends to cause harsh, non-selective oxidation and can nitrate aromatic rings. For controlled oxidation of a primary alcohol to an aldehyde, mild, anhydrous reagents like PCC or Dess-Martin periodinane are preferred.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Williamson ether synthesis involves the reaction of:
options: An alcohol with an alcohol using H₂SO₄
An alkoxide ion with a primary alkyl halide
A Grignard reagent with an epoxide
An alcohol with a carboxylic acid
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. An alkoxide ion with a primary alkyl halide

Williamson ether synthesis is an SN2 reaction between a sodium alkoxide (RO−Na⁺) and a primary alkyl halide (R'–X). This method gives good yields for unsymmetrical ethers. Secondary and tertiary halides favor elimination (E2) instead.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. An alcohol with an alcohol using H₂SO₄: Heating an alcohol with concentrated H2SO4 can produce an ether (especially symmetrical ethers at ~140 degrees C), but this is an acid-catalyzed condensation, not the Williamson synthesis. The Williamson method is superior because it can produce unsymmetrical ethers by choosing different alkoxide and alkyl halide partners.

C. A Grignard reagent with an epoxide: A Grignard reagent reacting with an epoxide does produce an alcohol with a longer carbon chain through ring-opening, but this is a Grignard reaction, not Williamson ether synthesis. The Williamson synthesis specifically uses an alkoxide nucleophile (RO-) attacking a primary alkyl halide (R'X) in an SN2 mechanism.

D. An alcohol with a carboxylic acid: An alcohol reacting with a carboxylic acid produces an ester (via Fischer esterification), not an ether. Esters have the general structure R-COO-R', while ethers have R-O-R'. The Williamson synthesis is an SN2 reaction, whereas esterification is a condensation/addition-elimination reaction.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: When ethanol reacts with sodium metal, the products are:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Sodium ethoxide and H₂

Sodium metal reacts with the O–H group of ethanol: 2CH₃CH₂OH + 2Na → 2CH₃CH₂ONa + H₂↑. This reaction is less vigorous than sodium with water, confirming that alcohols are weaker acids than water.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Sodium ethanoate and H₂: Sodium ethanoate (sodium acetate, CH3COONa) is the sodium salt of acetic acid, not ethanol. It would form if acetic acid (not ethanol) reacted with NaOH. When Na metal reacts with ethanol, it replaces the hydrogen of the -OH group to form sodium ethoxide (CH3CH2ONa), not a carboxylate salt.

C. Sodium ethoxide and H₂O: Sodium ethoxide and H2O is incorrect because the reaction produces H2 gas, not water. The sodium metal reduces the proton from the -OH group: 2 CH3CH2OH + 2 Na -> 2 CH3CH2ONa + H2 (gas). This is analogous to sodium reacting with water (2 Na + 2 H2O -> 2 NaOH + H2), but the reaction with ethanol is less vigorous.

D. Ethane and NaOH: Ethane (C2H6) and NaOH are not products of this reaction. Sodium reacts with the acidic O-H proton, not with a C-H bond. Breaking a C-H bond would require much more energy. The alkoxide (CH3CH2O- Na+) is the organic product, and hydrogen gas bubbles off.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following gives a positive result with FeCl₃ solution (violet/green coloration)?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Phenol

Phenol forms a colored complex with ferric chloride (FeCl₃), typically violet or green, due to the formation of an iron-phenoxide coordination compound. This is a characteristic test for enols and phenols. Simple alcohols and ethers do not give this reaction.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Ethanol: Ethanol is an aliphatic alcohol that does not give a color with FeCl3 solution. The FeCl3 test is specific to compounds with an enol or phenolic -OH group, which can form colored complexes with Fe3+ ions. Ethanol lacks the aromatic ring needed for this coordination.

B. Diethyl ether: Diethyl ether (CH3CH2-O-CH2CH3) has no -OH group at all, so it cannot form a complex with FeCl3. Ethers are generally quite unreactive, acting mainly as solvents. The FeCl3 test requires a phenolic hydroxyl that can chelate with the iron ion.

D. Methanol: Methanol, like ethanol, is an aliphatic alcohol and gives no color with FeCl3. Only compounds with a phenolic -OH group directly attached to an aromatic ring produce the characteristic violet, green, or blue coloration with ferric chloride. This makes the FeCl3 test a quick way to identify phenols.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The order of acidity among the following is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Phenol > Water > Ethanol

Phenol (pKₐ ≈ 10) is the most acidic due to resonance stabilization of the phenoxide ion. Water (pKₐ ≈ 15.7) is next. Ethanol (pKₐ ≈ 16) is the least acidic because the ethyl group is electron-donating (+I effect), destabilizing the ethoxide conjugate base.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Water > Ethanol > Phenol: Water > Ethanol > Phenol places phenol as the weakest acid, which is incorrect. Phenol (pKa ~10) is more acidic than water (pKa ~15.7) because the phenoxide ion is resonance-stabilized by the aromatic ring. The correct order of acidity is Phenol > Water > Ethanol.

C. Ethanol > Phenol > Water: Ethanol > Phenol > Water places ethanol as the strongest acid, which is backwards. Ethanol (pKa ~16) is actually the weakest acid of the three because the ethoxide ion is destabilized by the electron-donating ethyl group. Phenol is the strongest acid because of resonance stabilization of phenoxide.

D. Ethanol > Water > Phenol: Ethanol > Water > Phenol has the same error of placing ethanol as most acidic. Remember the pKa values: phenol (~10), water (~15.7), ethanol (~16). The key insight is that resonance stabilization of the conjugate base (phenoxide) dramatically increases acidity.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Acid-catalyzed ring opening of an epoxide by water produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: A. A diol (1,2-diol)

Epoxides (oxiranes) are highly strained three-membered rings. Under acid-catalyzed conditions, water acts as a nucleophile to open the ring, producing a 1,2-diol (vicinal diol). The reaction proceeds with Markovnikov-like regioselectivity under acidic conditions, attacking the more substituted carbon.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. An aldehyde: An aldehyde contains a -CHO group and would require oxidative cleavage of a C-C bond, which does not occur in simple epoxide ring-opening. Acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of an epoxide is a ring-opening reaction where water attacks one of the ring carbons, adding -OH groups to adjacent carbons to form a 1,2-diol (vicinal diol).

C. A ketone: A ketone contains a C=O group flanked by two carbon groups. Epoxide ring-opening with water does not create a carbonyl group; instead, it adds two hydroxyl groups to give a diol. Ketones can be formed from epoxides through different reagents, such as rearrangement with Lewis acids under specific conditions.

D. An ether: An ether has the structure R-O-R' and would result from an alcohol (not water) opening the epoxide ring. When water opens an epoxide, both new bonds formed are O-H bonds (giving -OH groups), not O-C bonds to another alkyl group. Using an alcohol as the nucleophile instead of water would indeed give a hydroxy ether.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Glycerol (propane-1,2,3-triol) is classified as:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. A trihydric alcohol

Glycerol (HOCH₂CH(OH)CH₂OH) contains three hydroxyl groups, making it a trihydric alcohol (triol). It has two primary –OH groups and one secondary –OH group. Glycerol is an important biological molecule as the backbone of triglycerides and phospholipids.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A monohydric primary alcohol: A monohydric alcohol has only one -OH group (like ethanol or methanol). Glycerol has three -OH groups on three consecutive carbons, making it trihydric. "Primary" refers to the carbon type bearing the -OH, not the number of hydroxyl groups. Glycerol actually has two primary -OH groups (on C1 and C3) and one secondary -OH (on C2).

B. A dihydric secondary alcohol: A dihydric alcohol (diol) has two -OH groups, like ethylene glycol (ethane-1,2-diol), which is used as antifreeze. Glycerol has three -OH groups, so it is trihydric. Additionally, calling it "secondary" is misleading since glycerol has both primary and secondary hydroxyl groups.

D. A phenol: A phenol has an -OH group directly bonded to a benzene ring. Glycerol is an aliphatic triol (propane-1,2,3-triol) with no aromatic ring. Phenols and alcohols differ significantly in acidity and chemical behavior because of the aromatic ring's electron effects on the -OH group.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which reagent is used in the Tollens' test to distinguish aldehydes from ketones?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Ammoniacal silver nitrate (Ag(NH₃)₂⁺)

Tollens' reagent contains the diamminesilver(I) complex [Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺. Aldehydes reduce Ag⁺ to metallic silver, forming a characteristic silver mirror on the test tube wall. Ketones do not react because they cannot be easily oxidized without breaking C–C bonds.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Fehling's solution: Fehling's solution (Cu2+ in alkaline tartrate) also tests for aldehydes (forming a brick-red Cu2O precipitate) but is a different reagent from Tollens'. Fehling's test is better suited for aliphatic aldehydes and reducing sugars. It uses copper(II) ions rather than silver ions, and the positive result is a red precipitate rather than a silver mirror.

C. Benedict's solution: Benedict's solution is similar to Fehling's, containing Cu2+ ions in alkaline citrate solution. It also detects reducing sugars and aldehydes by producing a color change from blue to green, yellow, orange, or red (depending on concentration). However, Benedict's is not the reagent used in the Tollens' test.

D. Iodine in NaOH: Iodine in NaOH (the iodoform test) detects methyl ketones (R-CO-CH3) and compounds that can be oxidized to methyl ketones (like secondary alcohols of the form R-CH(OH)-CH3). A positive iodoform test gives a yellow precipitate of CHI3. This test does not specifically distinguish aldehydes from ketones the way Tollens' test does.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The reaction of a ketone with hydroxylamine (NH₂OH) produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. An oxime

Hydroxylamine (NH₂OH) reacts with the carbonyl group of ketones (and aldehydes) through nucleophilic addition-elimination to form an oxime (C=N–OH). This is a condensation reaction with loss of water. Imines form with primary amines, hydrazones with hydrazine, and semicarbazones with semicarbazide.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. An imine (Schiff base): An imine (Schiff base) is formed when a ketone or aldehyde reacts with a primary amine (R-NH2), not hydroxylamine. The product has a C=N-R linkage. Schiff bases are important intermediates in biological chemistry, such as in the mechanism of transaminase enzymes and in the visual cycle (retinal + opsin).

C. A hydrazone: A hydrazone is formed when a ketone or aldehyde reacts with hydrazine (NH2-NH2) or a substituted hydrazine. The product has a C=N-NH2 (or C=N-NHR) linkage. 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine (Brady's reagent) reacts with carbonyl compounds to form orange/red 2,4-DNP hydrazones, which is a classic qualitative test.

D. A semicarbazone: A semicarbazone is formed when a ketone or aldehyde reacts with semicarbazide (NH2-NH-CO-NH2). These derivatives have sharp, characteristic melting points and were historically used to identify unknown carbonyl compounds before modern spectroscopy. Each nitrogen reagent (NH2OH, NH2NH2, semicarbazide) gives a different C=N derivative.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Aldol condensation of acetaldehyde produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. 3-hydroxybutanal

In aldol condensation, the enolate of one acetaldehyde molecule acts as a nucleophile, attacking the carbonyl carbon of another molecule. The initial product is a β-hydroxy aldehyde: 3-hydroxybutanal (aldol). Subsequent heating causes dehydration to form crotonaldehyde (but-2-enal), but the primary aldol product is 3-hydroxybutanal.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Acetone: Acetone (CH3COCH3) is a ketone and cannot be formed from the aldol condensation of acetaldehyde. The aldol reaction joins two acetaldehyde molecules: one provides the enolizable alpha-hydrogen, and the other provides the carbonyl electrophile. The product is an aldol (aldehyde + alcohol), specifically 3-hydroxybutanal.

C. Acetic acid: Acetic acid (CH3COOH) would be formed by oxidation of acetaldehyde, not by aldol condensation. The aldol reaction is a carbon-carbon bond-forming reaction, creating a beta-hydroxy aldehyde. No oxidation state change occurs at the carbonyl carbon in an aldol reaction.

D. Crotonaldehyde directly: Crotonaldehyde (2-butenal, CH3CH=CHCHO) is actually the dehydration product of 3-hydroxybutanal. The initial aldol product is 3-hydroxybutanal, which can then lose water (dehydrate) under heating to give crotonaldehyde (an alpha,beta-unsaturated aldehyde). The question asks about aldol condensation specifically, not the subsequent dehydration step.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following carbonyl compounds does NOT undergo the iodoform reaction?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Benzaldehyde

The iodoform reaction requires a methyl group adjacent to the carbonyl (CH₃CO– group) or a secondary alcohol that can be oxidized to such a structure. Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO), acetone (CH₃COCH₃), and methyl ethyl ketone (CH₃COCH₂CH₃) all have this feature. Benzaldehyde (C₆H₅CHO) lacks a methyl ketone group.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Acetaldehyde: Acetaldehyde (ch3cho), acetone (ch3coch3), and methyl ethyl ketone (ch3coch2ch3) all have this feature.

B. Acetone: Acetaldehyde (ch3cho), acetone (ch3coch3), and methyl ethyl ketone (ch3coch2ch3) all have this feature.

D. Methyl ethyl ketone: Acetaldehyde (ch3cho), acetone (ch3coch3), and methyl ethyl ketone (ch3coch2ch3) all have this feature.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The product of Grignard reaction between CH₃MgBr and formaldehyde (HCHO), after hydrolysis, is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Ethanol

The Grignard reagent CH₃MgBr adds to the carbonyl of formaldehyde (HCHO): CH₃MgBr + HCHO → CH₃CH₂OMgBr. Acidic hydrolysis gives ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH). Formaldehyde with a Grignard reagent always produces a primary alcohol.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Methanol: Methanol (CH3OH) has only one carbon and would result from reducing formaldehyde (HCHO) with NaBH4 or LiAlH4, not from a Grignard reaction. The Grignard reaction adds carbon atoms: CH3MgBr contributes one carbon to formaldehyde's one carbon, giving a two-carbon product (ethanol).

C. Propan-1-ol: Propan-1-ol (CH3CH2CH2OH) has three carbons and would form if CH3MgBr reacted with acetaldehyde (CH3CHO), not formaldehyde. Remember the Grignard carbon count: product carbons = carbons from RMgX + carbons from carbonyl compound. Here: 1 + 1 = 2 carbons = ethanol.

D. Propan-2-ol: Propan-2-ol (CH3CH(OH)CH3) has three carbons and would form if CH3MgBr reacted with acetone (CH3COCH3). Different carbonyl substrates give different alcohol types: formaldehyde gives primary alcohols, other aldehydes give secondary alcohols, and ketones also give secondary (or tertiary with excess) alcohols.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Cannizzaro reaction is characteristic of aldehydes that:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Lack α-hydrogens

The Cannizzaro reaction occurs with aldehydes that have no α-hydrogens (such as formaldehyde and benzaldehyde) when treated with concentrated NaOH. One molecule is oxidized to a carboxylate and the other is reduced to an alcohol (disproportionation). Aldehydes with α-hydrogens undergo aldol condensation instead.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Have α-hydrogens: Aldehydes with alpha-hydrogens preferentially undergo the aldol reaction (not Cannizzaro) in base. The alpha-hydrogen is abstracted to form an enolate, which attacks another carbonyl. The Cannizzaro reaction specifically occurs with aldehydes that lack alpha-hydrogens (like formaldehyde, benzaldehyde, or trimethylacetaldehyde) because they cannot form enolates.

C. Are unsaturated: Unsaturation (having double or triple bonds) is not the determining factor for the Cannizzaro reaction. The key requirement is the absence of alpha-hydrogens. An unsaturated aldehyde like cinnamaldehyde (C6H5CH=CHCHO) still has alpha-hydrogens and would prefer aldol chemistry over Cannizzaro.

D. Contain a methyl group: Containing a methyl group is not relevant to the Cannizzaro reaction. In fact, acetaldehyde (CH3CHO) has a methyl group with alpha-hydrogens and undergoes aldol condensation, not the Cannizzaro reaction. The Cannizzaro reaction is a disproportionation where one aldehyde molecule is oxidized to a carboxylate and another is reduced to an alcohol.

Key Educational Points:
