  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Reduction of a ketone with NaBH₄ produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. A secondary alcohol

NaBH₄ is a mild reducing agent that selectively reduces the carbonyl group. A ketone (R₁COR₂) is reduced to a secondary alcohol (R₁CH(OH)R₂) because the carbon bearing the new –OH group remains bonded to two other carbon-containing groups. Aldehyde reduction gives a primary alcohol.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A primary alcohol: Aldehyde reduction gives a primary alcohol.

C. A tertiary alcohol: A tertiary alcohol has three carbon groups attached to the carbon bearing the -OH. Reducing a ketone (which has two carbon groups on the carbonyl carbon) with NaBH4 adds one hydrogen, giving a carbon with two carbon groups, one hydrogen, and one -OH. That is a secondary alcohol, not tertiary. Tertiary alcohols cannot be made by simple carbonyl reduction.

D. An alkane: An alkane would result from complete reduction that removes the oxygen entirely. NaBH4 is a mild reducing agent that reduces the C=O double bond to C-OH (adding H across the C=O bond) but does not remove the oxygen atom. Stronger conditions, like Clemmensen reduction (Zn-Hg/HCl) or Wolff-Kishner reduction (NH2NH2/KOH), can fully reduce a carbonyl to a -CH2- group.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following is the correct order of reactivity toward nucleophilic addition?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Formaldehyde > Acetaldehyde > Acetone

Reactivity toward nucleophilic addition decreases with increasing steric hindrance and increasing electron donation to the carbonyl carbon. Formaldehyde (HCHO) has the least steric hindrance and no electron-donating alkyl groups. Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) has one methyl group, and acetone (CH₃COCH₃) has two, making it the least reactive.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Acetone > Formaldehyde > Acetaldehyde: Acetone > Formaldehyde > Acetaldehyde is the reverse of the correct trend. Acetone is the least reactive toward nucleophilic addition because its two methyl groups create the most steric hindrance around the carbonyl carbon and donate the most electron density (reducing electrophilicity). Formaldehyde, with no alkyl groups, is the most reactive.

C. Acetaldehyde > Formaldehyde > Acetone: Acetaldehyde > Formaldehyde > Acetone incorrectly places acetaldehyde as most reactive. Formaldehyde (HCHO) has the highest reactivity because its carbonyl carbon has two small hydrogen atoms, offering minimal steric hindrance and no electron-donating alkyl groups. Reactivity decreases as more alkyl groups are attached: HCHO > CH3CHO > (CH3)2CO.

D. Acetone > Acetaldehyde > Formaldehyde: Acetone > Acetaldehyde > Formaldehyde is exactly backwards. Two factors determine nucleophilic addition reactivity: (1) steric effects, with more groups around the carbonyl making nucleophilic approach harder, and (2) electronic effects, with electron-donating alkyl groups reducing the partial positive charge on carbon. Both factors favor formaldehyde as most reactive.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The reaction of acetone with HCN produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: A. Acetone cyanohydrin

HCN adds across the carbonyl of acetone via nucleophilic addition. The CN− ion attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon, and subsequent protonation gives the cyanohydrin: (CH₃)₂C(OH)(CN). Cyanohydrin formation is a key reaction for extending the carbon chain and introducing functional groups.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. Acetonitrile: Acetonitrile (CH3CN) is a nitrile formed by dehydration of acetamide or by reaction of methyl halide with cyanide, not by HCN addition to a ketone. The cyanohydrin reaction is a nucleophilic addition where CN- attacks the carbonyl carbon and H+ protonates the oxygen, giving (CH3)2C(OH)(CN), which is acetone cyanohydrin.

C. Lactic acid: Lactic acid (2-hydroxypropanoic acid) would require hydrolysis of the cyanohydrin. First acetone cyanohydrin forms, and then if the nitrile group is hydrolyzed (by acid or base), it converts to a carboxylic acid. So lactic acid could be a downstream product, but the immediate product of acetone + HCN is the cyanohydrin.

D. Acrylonitrile: Acrylonitrile (CH2=CHCN) is an unsaturated nitrile used in polymer production (polyacrylonitrile). It is made industrially by ammoxidation of propene, not by adding HCN to acetone. The HCN addition to a carbonyl is a nucleophilic addition reaction, preserving the carbon-oxygen bond as a hydroxyl group.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Treatment of an aldehyde with excess alcohol in the presence of acid catalyst gives:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. An acetal

One equivalent of alcohol reacts with an aldehyde to form a hemiacetal (unstable intermediate with –OH and –OR on the same carbon). With excess alcohol and acid catalyst, a second molecule of alcohol replaces the –OH, forming a stable acetal (R–CH(OR')₂). Acetals are used as protecting groups for carbonyls in organic synthesis.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A hemiacetal: A hemiacetal forms when one equivalent of alcohol adds to an aldehyde, giving R-CH(OH)(OR'). With excess alcohol and acid catalyst, the reaction proceeds further: the hemiacetal loses water and adds a second alcohol molecule to give the acetal R-CH(OR')2. Hemiacetals are unstable intermediates on the way to acetals.

C. An ester: An ester (R-COOR') is formed by the reaction of a carboxylic acid (not an aldehyde) with an alcohol under acid catalysis (Fischer esterification). The aldehyde carbonyl carbon is at a lower oxidation state than a carboxylic acid, so the product of aldehyde + alcohol is an acetal, not an ester.

D. An enol ether: An enol ether (vinyl ether, R-O-CR'=CR'') is a different functional group formed by elimination reactions, not by alcohol addition to an aldehyde. Acetals (R-CH(OR')2) are geminal diethers, with two -OR' groups on the same carbon, and serve as important protecting groups for aldehydes in organic synthesis.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following carboxylic acids is the strongest?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: D. Cl₃CCOOH

Electron-withdrawing groups (like Cl) stabilize the carboxylate conjugate base through the inductive effect (-I). More chlorine atoms increase the electron-withdrawing power, stabilizing the negative charge more effectively. Thus acidity increases: CH₃COOH < ClCH₂COOH < Cl₂CHCOOH < Cl₃CCOOH (trichloroacetic acid, pKₐ ≈ 0.65).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. CH₃COOH: Acetic acid (CH3COOH, pKa 4.76) is the weakest acid in this series because the methyl group is slightly electron-donating, which destabilizes the carboxylate anion. With no electron-withdrawing substituents, acetic acid serves as the baseline for comparing substituent effects on acidity.

B. ClCH₂COOH: Chloroacetic acid (ClCH2COOH, pKa 2.86) is stronger than acetic acid because the chlorine atom withdraws electron density through the inductive effect, stabilizing the carboxylate anion. However, it has only one chlorine, so its inductive effect is weaker than that of dichloro- or trichloroacetic acid.

C. Cl₂CHCOOH: Dichloroacetic acid (Cl2CHCOOH, pKa 1.29) is stronger than monochloroacetic acid because two chlorine atoms exert a greater electron-withdrawing inductive effect. However, trichloroacetic acid (Cl3CCOOH, pKa 0.65) is even stronger because three chlorines provide the maximum inductive withdrawal in this series.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Fischer esterification involves the reaction of a carboxylic acid with an alcohol in the presence of:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. An acid catalyst (e.g., H₂SO₄)

Fischer esterification is an acid-catalyzed condensation of a carboxylic acid and an alcohol to form an ester and water: RCOOH + R'OH ⇌ RCOOR' + H₂O. The acid catalyst protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbon more electrophilic. The reaction is reversible, so excess alcohol or removal of water drives it to completion.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A base catalyst: A base catalyst would actually promote hydrolysis of an ester (saponification), which is the reverse of esterification. Fischer esterification requires an acid catalyst (typically H2SO4 or p-toluenesulfonic acid) to protonate the carbonyl oxygen, making the carboxylic acid more electrophilic for nucleophilic attack by the alcohol.

C. NaOH: NaOH is a strong base that would deprotonate the carboxylic acid to form a carboxylate salt (RCOO- Na+), which is unreactive toward nucleophilic attack by an alcohol. In fact, NaOH is the reagent used for ester hydrolysis (saponification), which is the opposite of esterification.

D. LiAlH₄: LiAlH4 is a powerful reducing agent that would reduce the carboxylic acid to a primary alcohol, not catalyze ester formation. Mixing a carboxylic acid with LiAlH4 would give two alcohol molecules, not an ester. LiAlH4 reductions must be carried out in anhydrous ether, not in the presence of acidic protons.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The correct order of reactivity of carboxylic acid derivatives toward nucleophilic acyl substitution is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Acid chloride > Anhydride > Ester > Amide

Reactivity depends on the leaving group ability and the electron donation to the carbonyl. Acid chlorides are most reactive (Cl− is a good leaving group, minimal resonance stabilization). Amides are least reactive because the nitrogen lone pair strongly stabilizes the carbonyl through resonance, and –NH₂ is a poor leaving group.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Ester > Amide > Acid chloride > Anhydride: Ester > Amide > Acid chloride > Anhydride is essentially the reverse of the correct order. Reactivity in nucleophilic acyl substitution depends on the leaving group ability: Cl- (excellent) > RCOO- (good) > RO- (moderate) > NH2-/NHR- (poor). Better leaving groups mean higher reactivity.

C. Amide > Ester > Anhydride > Acid chloride: Amide > Ester > Anhydride > Acid chloride is also backwards. Amides are the least reactive carboxylic acid derivatives because nitrogen donates its lone pair into the carbonyl by resonance, stabilizing the C=O and making it resistant to nucleophilic attack. This is why peptide bonds (amide bonds) are kinetically stable in proteins.

D. Anhydride > Acid chloride > Amide > Ester: Anhydride > Acid chloride > Amide > Ester incorrectly places anhydrides above acid chlorides. Acid chlorides are the most reactive because Cl- is the best leaving group (weakest base) among the four. The full reactivity order is: acid chloride > anhydride > ester > amide, corresponding to decreasing leaving group ability.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Hydrolysis of ethyl ethanoate (ethyl acetate) with NaOH is called:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Saponification

Base-catalyzed hydrolysis of an ester is called saponification. NaOH attacks the ester carbonyl, giving a carboxylate salt and an alcohol: CH₃COOCH₂CH₃ + NaOH → CH₃COO−Na⁺ + CH₃CH₂OH. Unlike acid hydrolysis, saponification is irreversible because the carboxylate anion is very stable.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Esterification: Esterification is the formation of an ester from an acid and alcohol, which is the reverse of what this question describes. The question describes ester hydrolysis (breaking the ester bond), not ester formation. Fischer esterification (acid-catalyzed) makes esters; saponification (base-catalyzed hydrolysis) breaks them.

C. Transesterification: Transesterification is the exchange of the alkoxy group of an ester with a different alcohol (ester + alcohol -> new ester + new alcohol). This is not hydrolysis because no water or hydroxide is involved in the key step. Transesterification is used industrially to make biodiesel from vegetable oils.

D. Decarboxylation: Decarboxylation is the loss of CO2 from a carboxylic acid or its derivative. It has nothing to do with ester hydrolysis. An example of decarboxylation is heating a beta-keto acid, which loses CO2 to give a ketone. Saponification, by contrast, breaks the ester into a carboxylate salt and an alcohol.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Decarboxylation of sodium acetate with soda lime (NaOH/CaO) produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Methane

Heating sodium acetate (CH₃COONa) with soda lime (NaOH + CaO) causes decarboxylation: CH₃COONa + NaOH → CH₄ + Na₂CO₃. The product alkane has one fewer carbon than the original acid. This is a classic method for preparing methane in the laboratory.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Ethane: Ethane (C2H6) has two carbons, but the decarboxylation of sodium acetate (CH3COONa) removes the carboxyl carbon as carbonate, leaving only one carbon (CH3-). Combined with a hydrogen (from NaOH), this gives methane (CH4). The product always has one fewer carbon than the starting carboxylate.

C. Ethene: Ethene (C2H4) is an unsaturated hydrocarbon that is not produced in this reaction. Soda lime decarboxylation (heating RCOONa with NaOH/CaO) replaces the -COONa group with -H, yielding an alkane with one fewer carbon. No elimination occurs, so unsaturated products do not form.

D. Acetone: Acetone ((CH3)2CO) has three carbons and a carbonyl group. The decarboxylation of sodium acetate is a straightforward loss of CO2 (as Na2CO3), producing the simplest alkane, methane. Acetone can be formed by dry distillation of calcium acetate, which is a different reaction entirely.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following is an anhydride?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. (CH₃CO)₂O

Acetic anhydride (CH₃CO)₂O has the general structure of two acyl groups bonded to the same oxygen (RCO–O–COR). CH₃COOCH₃ is an ester (methyl acetate), CH₃CONHCH₃ is an amide (N-methylacetamide), and CH₃COCl is an acid chloride (acetyl chloride).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. CH₃COOCH₃: CH3COOCH3 is methyl acetate, an ester (not an anhydride). Esters have the general structure R-COO-R', where an alkyl group replaces the acidic hydrogen of a carboxylic acid. An anhydride, by contrast, has two acyl groups linked by an oxygen: R-CO-O-CO-R', which can be thought of as two carboxylic acid molecules minus one water.

B. CH₃CONHCH₃: CH3CONHCH3 is N-methylacetamide, an amide. Amides have the structure R-CO-NHR' (or R-CO-NH2 for primary amides). The N-H bond in amides gives them high boiling points and makes them resistant to hydrolysis. Amides are the least reactive carboxylic acid derivatives.

D. CH₃COCl: CH3COCl is acetyl chloride, an acid chloride (acyl chloride). Acid chlorides have the structure R-CO-Cl and are the most reactive carboxylic acid derivatives. They react vigorously with water, alcohols, and amines. The four common carboxylic acid derivatives are: acid chlorides, anhydrides, esters, and amides.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Aspirin is produced by the acetylation of salicylic acid with:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Acetic anhydride

Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is synthesized by treating salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid) with acetic anhydride. The phenolic –OH group is acetylated to form an ester linkage. Acetic anhydride is preferred over acetic acid because the reaction is faster and more complete.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Acetic acid: Acetic acid (CH3COOH) could theoretically acetylate the -OH group of salicylic acid, but the reaction would be extremely slow and give poor yields without special conditions. Acetic anhydride is preferred because it is more reactive (a better acetylating agent) and the reaction proceeds cleanly at mild temperatures.

C. Acetaldehyde: Acetaldehyde (CH3CHO) is an aldehyde, not an acetylating agent. It would react with the -OH group of salicylic acid to form a hemiacetal, not an ester. Aspirin synthesis requires acetylation (transferring an acetyl group, CH3CO-), which anhydrides and acid chlorides can accomplish but aldehydes cannot.

D. Acetone: Acetone (CH3COCH3) is a ketone that does not serve as an acetylating agent. Ketones are relatively unreactive toward nucleophilic acyl substitution because they lack a good leaving group. To make aspirin, you need acetic anhydride (or acetyl chloride), which can donate an acetyl group to the phenolic -OH of salicylic acid.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which carboxylic acid derivative has the highest boiling point?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Butanamide (MW 87)

Amides have very high boiling points due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding. The N–H bonds in primary amides can form multiple hydrogen bonds (both as donors and acceptors through the C=O). Carboxylic acids form dimers through H-bonding but have lower MW here. Esters and acid chlorides cannot donate H-bonds as effectively.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Ethyl acetate (MW 88): Ethyl acetate (bp ~77 degrees C) is an ester with moderate polarity. Esters can act as hydrogen bond acceptors but not donors, so their boiling points are lower than those of comparable carboxylic acids or amides. Ethyl acetate is a common solvent with a characteristic fruity smell.

B. Propionic acid (MW 74): Propionic acid (bp ~141 degrees C) has strong hydrogen bonding (both donor and acceptor through its -COOH group) and actually forms dimers in the liquid phase. However, amides have even stronger hydrogen bonding due to the N-H bond, giving butanamide a higher boiling point despite similar molecular weight.

D. Butanoyl chloride (MW 107): Butanoyl chloride (bp ~102 degrees C) has the highest molecular weight in this set but the second-lowest boiling point. Acid chlorides are polar but cannot form strong hydrogen bonds because they lack N-H or O-H groups. This illustrates that hydrogen bonding ability trumps molecular weight in determining boiling point.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) reaction introduces a bromine at which position of a carboxylic acid?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. The α-carbon

The HVZ reaction uses Br₂ with a catalytic amount of PBr₃ (or red phosphorus) to selectively brominate the α-carbon of a carboxylic acid. The mechanism involves formation of an acid bromide, which enolizes more readily than the acid itself, followed by α-bromination. The product is an α-bromo carboxylic acid.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. The carbonyl carbon: Bromine does not substitute at the carbonyl carbon in the HVZ reaction. The carbonyl carbon is already at a high oxidation state (bonded to oxygen). The HVZ mechanism involves converting the acid to an acyl bromide, then forming an enol, which brominates at the α-carbon (the carbon adjacent to the carbonyl).

C. The β-carbon: The β-carbon (two carbons away from the carbonyl) is not the site of bromination in the HVZ reaction. Alpha-bromination is favored because the enol intermediate places a reactive double bond between the α-carbon and the carbonyl carbon, making the alpha position the site of electrophilic attack by bromine.

D. The terminal carbon: The terminal carbon (the end of the chain farthest from the carbonyl) is not selectively brominated by the HVZ reaction. Selective terminal functionalization is quite difficult in organic chemistry. The HVZ reaction is specifically useful because it provides a reliable method for alpha-bromination of carboxylic acids.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Reduction of a carboxylic acid with LiAlH₄ produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. A primary alcohol

LiAlH₄ is a powerful reducing agent that reduces carboxylic acids (RCOOH) all the way to primary alcohols (RCH₂OH). The reduction proceeds through an aldehyde intermediate, but LiAlH₄ is strong enough to reduce that as well. NaBH₄ alone cannot reduce carboxylic acids.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. An aldehyde: The reduction proceeds through an aldehyde intermediate, but lialh4 is strong enough to reduce that as well.

C. A secondary alcohol: A secondary alcohol has two carbon substituents on the carbon bearing the -OH. Reducing a carboxylic acid (R-COOH) gives R-CH2OH, which is a primary alcohol (only one carbon substituent on the -OH-bearing carbon). Secondary alcohols are formed by reducing ketones, not carboxylic acids.

D. An alkane: An alkane would result from complete removal of the oxygen, which LiAlH4 does not accomplish. LiAlH4 reduces the carboxylic acid through two successive reductions: first to an aldehyde (which is not isolable in this reaction), then further to the primary alcohol. To remove oxygen entirely, you would need Clemmensen or Wolff-Kishner reduction of a ketone or aldehyde.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Brønsted-Lowry definition: acids donate protons (H+), bases accept protons. The Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate them.
  • pH = -log[H+]; a change of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in H+ concentration. Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45.
  • Buffer systems (e.g., bicarbonate/carbonic acid in blood) resist pH changes by absorbing excess H+ or OH- ions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The correct order of basicity is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. (CH₃)₂NH > CH₃NH₂ > NH₃

In aqueous solution, basicity of amines is determined by the electron-donating inductive effect of alkyl groups and solvation. Dimethylamine is the most basic due to two electron-donating methyl groups increasing electron density on nitrogen, while still being well solvated. Trimethylamine is slightly less basic than dimethylamine due to steric hindrance to solvation despite having three methyl groups.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. NH₃ > CH₃NH₂ > (CH₃)₂NH: NH3 > CH3NH2 > (CH3)2NH has the order exactly reversed. In the gas phase and in general, adding alkyl groups to nitrogen increases basicity because alkyl groups are electron-donating (through induction), making the lone pair on nitrogen more available for protonation. So NH3 is the weakest and (CH3)2NH is the strongest in this series.

C. CH₃NH₂ > (CH₃)₂NH > NH₃: CH3NH2 > (CH3)2NH > NH3 incorrectly places methylamine above dimethylamine. The second methyl group in dimethylamine further increases electron density on nitrogen, making it a stronger base. In aqueous solution, (CH3)2NH is indeed more basic than CH3NH2 due to both inductive effects and favorable solvation of its conjugate acid.

D. (CH₃)₃N > (CH₃)₂NH > CH₃NH₂: (CH3)3N > (CH3)2NH > CH3NH2 seems logical (more alkyl groups = more basic), but in aqueous solution, trimethylamine is actually slightly less basic than dimethylamine. This is because the trimethylammonium ion (CH3)3NH+ has no N-H bonds available for stabilizing hydrogen bonds with water, making its conjugate acid less stable in water (the steric/solvation effect).

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexylamine because:
options: Aniline has a higher molecular weight
The nitrogen lone pair in aniline is delocalized into the benzene ring
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. The nitrogen lone pair in aniline is delocalized into the benzene ring

In aniline, the nitrogen lone pair participates in resonance with the aromatic π system, reducing its availability for protonation. In cyclohexylamine, no such delocalization occurs (the ring is saturated), so the nitrogen lone pair is fully available as a base. This makes cyclohexylamine significantly more basic than aniline.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Aniline has a higher molecular weight: Molecular weight has no meaningful effect on basicity. Basicity depends on the availability of the nitrogen lone pair for accepting a proton. In aniline, the lone pair is partially delocalized into the benzene ring through resonance, making it less available for protonation. This is a purely electronic effect, not related to molecular size.

C. Aniline has more hydrogen atoms: Aniline actually has fewer hydrogen atoms than cyclohexylamine, but this is irrelevant to basicity. The number of hydrogens on a molecule does not determine how readily the nitrogen lone pair accepts a proton. The key factor is lone pair delocalization: in cyclohexylamine, the lone pair is fully localized on nitrogen and readily available for protonation.

D. Cyclohexylamine has a phenyl group: This statement has the structures confused. Cyclohexylamine has a cyclohexyl group (saturated ring), not a phenyl group (aromatic ring). Aniline is the one with the phenyl group. The aromatic ring in aniline is what reduces basicity by delocalizing the nitrogen lone pair through resonance, which cannot happen with the saturated cyclohexane ring.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which amino acid has a nonpolar, hydrophobic side chain?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Leucine

Leucine has an isobutyl side chain (–CH₂CH(CH₃)₂), which is entirely hydrocarbon and therefore nonpolar and hydrophobic. Serine has a hydroxyl group (polar), aspartate has a carboxyl group (charged), and lysine has an amino group (charged at physiological pH).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Serine: Serine has a hydroxyl group (polar), aspartate has a carboxyl group (charged), and lysine has an amino group (charged at physiological ph).

B. Aspartate: Serine has a hydroxyl group (polar), aspartate has a carboxyl group (charged), and lysine has an amino group (charged at physiological ph).

D. Lysine: Serine has a hydroxyl group (polar), aspartate has a carboxyl group (charged), and lysine has an amino group (charged at physiological ph).

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: At its isoelectric point, an amino acid exists predominantly as:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. A zwitterion

At the isoelectric point (pI), the amino acid carries no net charge but exists as a zwitterion — a dipolar ion with the amino group protonated (–NH₃⁺) and the carboxyl group deprotonated (–COO−). At this pH, the amino acid does not migrate in an electric field.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A cation: A cation form (net positive charge) exists when the pH is well below the pI. At very low pH, the amino group is protonated (-NH3+) and the carboxyl group remains protonated (-COOH), giving a net positive charge. As pH rises toward the pI, the carboxyl group deprotonates and the net charge reaches zero.

B. An anion: An anion form (net negative charge) exists when the pH is well above the pI. At very high pH, both groups are deprotonated: -NH2 and -COO-, giving a net negative charge. At the isoelectric point, the opposite charges balance out to give zero net charge in the zwitterionic form.

D. An uncharged molecule: An "uncharged molecule" would imply that no functional groups carry any charge. This is incorrect because at the isoelectric point, the amino acid exists as a zwitterion where both groups are charged (-NH3+ and -COO-), but the charges cancel. The zwitterionic form is distinct from a truly uncharged molecule and explains why amino acids have high melting points and good water solubility.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The peptide bond between two amino acids is:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. An amide bond

A peptide bond is formed by a condensation reaction between the α-amino group of one amino acid and the α-carboxyl group of another, releasing water. The resulting –CO–NH– linkage is an amide bond. It has partial double-bond character due to resonance, making it planar and restricting rotation.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. An ester bond: An ester bond (R-COO-R') links an acid and an alcohol. In biochemistry, ester bonds connect fatty acids to glycerol in triglycerides and link nucleotides in DNA/RNA (phosphodiester bonds). The peptide bond, however, is formed between an amino group and a carboxyl group, producing an amide linkage (R-CO-NH-R'), not an ester.

B. A glycosidic bond: A glycosidic bond connects sugars to other sugars or to non-sugar molecules (aglycones). It is an acetal-type linkage found in disaccharides (like sucrose and lactose) and polysaccharides (like starch and cellulose). Glycosidic bonds involve the anomeric carbon of a sugar, which is entirely different from the C-N bond in a peptide.

D. A disulfide bond: A disulfide bond (S-S) links two cysteine residues through oxidation of their thiol (-SH) groups. Disulfide bonds stabilize protein tertiary and quaternary structure but are not part of the protein backbone. The backbone is held together exclusively by peptide (amide) bonds between consecutive amino acids.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Treatment of a primary amine with nitrous acid (HNO₂) at 0-5°C gives:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. A diazonium salt

Primary aromatic amines react with HNO₂ (generated from NaNO₂ + HCl) at 0–5°C to form relatively stable diazonium salts (ArN₂⁺Cl−). These are important intermediates in organic synthesis for Sandmeyer reactions and azo dye formation. Primary aliphatic amines also form diazonium salts, but they decompose rapidly to give nitrogen gas and carbocations.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A secondary amine: A secondary amine (R2NH) reacting with nitrous acid gives an N-nitrosamine (R2N-N=O), a yellow oily compound, not a diazonium salt. The reaction of primary amines with HNO2 is unique in forming diazonium salts (R-N2+ X-) because primary amines have two N-H bonds available for the reaction mechanism.

C. A nitrile: A nitrile (R-CN) is formed by dehydrating primary amides or by nucleophilic substitution with cyanide. It is not a product of primary amine + HNO2. The diazonium salt formed in this reaction is a versatile synthetic intermediate that can be converted to phenols, halides, nitriles (via Sandmeyer reaction), and azo dyes.

D. An amide: An amide (R-CO-NH2) is formed by reacting an amine with an acyl derivative (acid chloride, anhydride, etc.), not with nitrous acid. The reaction of a primary aromatic amine with HNO2 at 0-5 degrees C produces a diazonium salt, which must be kept cold to prevent decomposition (loss of N2 gas).

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Gabriel synthesis is used to prepare:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Primary amines

The Gabriel synthesis uses potassium phthalimide, which acts as a nucleophile in an SN2 reaction with a primary alkyl halide. Subsequent hydrolysis (or treatment with hydrazine — Ing-Manske procedure) releases the primary amine. This method avoids over-alkylation problems because the nitrogen in phthalimide is only monoalkylated.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Secondary amines: Gabriel synthesis cannot produce secondary amines because the phthalimide nitrogen can only be alkylated once before hydrolysis. The method involves potassium phthalimide reacting with a primary alkyl halide (SN2), then hydrolysis releases the primary amine. For secondary amines, other methods like reductive amination are used.

B. Tertiary amines: Tertiary amines cannot be made by Gabriel synthesis for the same reason. The phthalimide nitrogen is already bonded to two carbonyl carbons, so only one alkyl group can be introduced. Gabriel synthesis is valued precisely because it avoids the over-alkylation problem that plagues direct amine synthesis.

D. Quaternary ammonium salts: Quaternary ammonium salts (R4N+) have four alkyl groups on nitrogen and a positive charge. Gabriel synthesis produces only primary amines (one alkyl group on the free nitrogen). Making quaternary salts requires exhaustive alkylation of an amine, which is a completely different approach.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following amino acids contains sulfur?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Cysteine

Cysteine contains a thiol (–SH) group in its side chain (HSCH₂–). Two cysteine residues can form a disulfide bond (–S–S–) called cystine, which is important for stabilizing protein tertiary and quaternary structure. Methionine is the other sulfur-containing amino acid (thioether).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Valine: Valine is a branched-chain, nonpolar amino acid with the side chain -CH(CH3)2. It contains only carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen, with no sulfur. Valine is one of the three branched-chain amino acids (along with leucine and isoleucine) that are essential in human nutrition.

C. Tyrosine: Tyrosine has an aromatic side chain (a phenol group, 4-hydroxyphenyl). It contains no sulfur. Tyrosine is important as a precursor to several key biomolecules: dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine, thyroid hormones, and melanin.

D. Histidine: Histidine has an imidazole ring in its side chain, containing nitrogen atoms but no sulfur. The two sulfur-containing standard amino acids are cysteine (with a free thiol -SH group that forms disulfide bonds) and methionine (with a thioether -S-CH3 group that serves as the initiator amino acid in translation).

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Hofmann elimination of a quaternary ammonium hydroxide yields primarily:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. The least substituted alkene (Hofmann product)

Hofmann elimination preferentially forms the less substituted (less stable) alkene, which is the opposite of Zaitsev's rule. The bulky trimethylammonium leaving group creates steric effects that favor removal of the more accessible (less hindered) β-hydrogen, giving the Hofmann product.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. The most substituted alkene (Zaitsev product): The Zaitsev product (most substituted alkene) is the major product in most E2 eliminations with strong bases and typical leaving groups. However, Hofmann elimination is an exception: the bulky trimethylamine leaving group creates steric hindrance that favors proton removal from the least hindered position, giving the less substituted (Hofmann) alkene as the major product.

C. An alcohol: An alcohol is not a product of Hofmann elimination. The reaction converts a quaternary ammonium hydroxide into an alkene and a tertiary amine (trimethylamine) upon heating. The hydroxide acts as the base that abstracts a beta-hydrogen. No alcohol is formed in this elimination process.

D. An amine oxide: An amine oxide is produced by a different reaction: oxidation of a tertiary amine with a peroxide (like H2O2 or mCPBA). Amine oxide elimination (Cope elimination) is a related but distinct reaction. Hofmann elimination specifically involves heating a quaternary ammonium hydroxide to produce the least substituted alkene.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The Hinsberg test distinguishes between primary, secondary, and tertiary amines using:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Benzenesulfonyl chloride (C₆H₅SO₂Cl)

In the Hinsberg test, primary amines form sulfonamides soluble in NaOH (due to the acidic N–H). Secondary amines form sulfonamides insoluble in NaOH (no N–H to deprotonate). Tertiary amines do not react with sulfonyl chloride because they lack an N–H bond. This differential behavior allows classification.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. FeCl₃ solution: FeCl3 solution is used to test for phenols (giving a characteristic violet, green, or blue color due to complex formation with Fe3+ ions) and for enols. It does not distinguish between amine classes. The Hinsberg test specifically uses benzenesulfonyl chloride, which reacts differently with primary, secondary, and tertiary amines.

C. Tollens' reagent: Tollens' reagent (ammoniacal AgNO3) is used to detect aldehydes by oxidizing them to carboxylates while reducing Ag+ to metallic silver (the "silver mirror" test). It has no application in amine classification. The Hinsberg test works because primary amines form soluble sulfonamides in NaOH, secondary amines form insoluble ones, and tertiary amines do not react.

D. Ninhydrin solution: Ninhydrin solution is used to detect alpha-amino acids and primary amines, producing a characteristic purple color (Ruhemann's purple). While ninhydrin does react with amines, it does not distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary amines the way the Hinsberg test does.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: How many structural isomers does C₄H₁₀ have?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. 2

C₄H₁₀ (butane) has two structural isomers: n-butane (CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₃) with a straight chain, and isobutane or 2-methylpropane (CH(CH₃)₃) with a branched chain. These differ in the connectivity of their carbon atoms.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 1: One isomer would mean C4H10 has only one possible structure, but there are two: n-butane (a straight chain of four carbons, CH3CH2CH2CH3) and isobutane (2-methylpropane, (CH3)3CH, with a branched structure). Even with just four carbons, branching creates a second structural isomer.

C. 3: Three structural isomers is incorrect for C4H10. There are only two ways to arrange four carbon atoms: as a straight chain (butane) or with one branch (2-methylpropane). The number of structural isomers grows rapidly with carbon count: C5H12 has 3, C6H14 has 5, C7H16 has 9, and C10H22 has 75.

D. 4: Four structural isomers is too many for C4H10. With only four carbons, the branching options are very limited. You can have a straight chain or a single methyl branch (there is only one position for it). Compare this to C5H12, which has 3 isomers: pentane, 2-methylbutane, and 2,2-dimethylpropane (neopentane).

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Cis-trans isomerism in alkenes requires:
options: Two different substituents on each carbon of the double bond
Free rotation about the double bond
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Two different substituents on each carbon of the double bond

Geometric (cis-trans) isomerism occurs when each carbon of the C=C double bond bears two different groups, and the restricted rotation prevents interconversion. If either carbon has two identical substituents, the compound cannot exhibit cis-trans isomerism.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A triple bond: A triple bond does not exhibit cis-trans isomerism. In a triple bond (like in alkynes), each carbon is sp-hybridized with a linear geometry (180 degrees), leaving no possibility for different spatial arrangements. Cis-trans isomerism requires restricted rotation, which a double bond provides, along with different substituents on each carbon.

C. Free rotation about the double bond: Free rotation about the double bond is actually the reason cis-trans isomerism does NOT occur in single bonds. The C=C double bond has restricted rotation due to the π bond above and below the plane. If there were free rotation, the cis and trans forms would interconvert freely and would not be isolable isomers.

D. An asymmetric carbon: An asymmetric (chiral) carbon is required for enantiomerism (optical isomerism), not for cis-trans isomerism. Cis-trans isomers are a type of diastereomer arising from restricted rotation around double bonds or in cyclic structures. A molecule can show cis-trans isomerism without having any chiral centers at all.

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: A molecule with one chiral center has how many stereoisomers?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. 2

A molecule with one chiral center (asymmetric carbon) exists as a pair of enantiomers — non-superimposable mirror images. The maximum number of stereoisomers is 2ⁿ where n is the number of chiral centers. With n = 1, there are 2¹ = 2 stereoisomers (the R and S configurations).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 1: A single molecule cannot be its own stereoisomer. The formula 2ⁿ (where n = number of chiral centers) gives 2¹ = 2 stereoisomers — one R and one S enantiomer. Saying there is only 1 stereoisomer would mean the molecule has no mirror image, which contradicts having a chiral center.

C. 3: Three stereoisomers would require an odd number from the 2ⁿ formula, which is impossible since 2 raised to any whole number is always even. An odd number of stereoisomers can arise only in special cases like meso compounds reducing the total, but that still would not yield 3 from a single chiral center.

D. 4: Four stereoisomers (2² = 4) would require two chiral centers. For each additional chiral center, the maximum number of stereoisomers doubles. A molecule with one chiral center can only exist as two enantiomers (R and S).

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which molecule can exhibit optical activity?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Butan-2-ol

Butan-2-ol (CH₃CH(OH)CH₂CH₃) has a chiral center at C2, bonded to four different groups: –H, –OH, –CH₃, and –CH₂CH₃. This asymmetry allows it to rotate plane-polarized light. The other molecules either lack four different substituents on any carbon (no chirality center) or are too symmetric.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Propan-2-ol: Propan-2-ol (CH₃CH(OH)CH₃) has a central carbon bonded to –OH, –H, and two identical –CH₃ groups. Since two substituents are the same, this carbon is not a chiral center, so propan-2-ol cannot rotate plane-polarized light.

C. 2-methylpropan-2-ol: 2-Methylpropan-2-ol ((CH₃)₃COH) is a tertiary alcohol where the central carbon bears three identical methyl groups plus one –OH. With three of four substituents identical, there is no chiral center and no optical activity.

D. Methanol: Methanol (CH₃OH) is the simplest alcohol. Its carbon is bonded to three hydrogen atoms and one –OH group, giving it no chiral center. A carbon must have four different substituents to be a stereocenter.

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Meso compounds are optically inactive despite having chiral centers because they:
options: Have no chiral centers at all
Possess an internal plane of symmetry
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Possess an internal plane of symmetry

A meso compound contains chiral centers but has an internal plane of symmetry (or other symmetry element) that makes the molecule achiral overall. The optical rotation from one chiral center is exactly cancelled by the other. For example, meso-tartaric acid has two chiral centers but is optically inactive.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Have no chiral centers at all: This is incorrect because meso compounds do have chiral centers — they typically have two or more. What makes them special is that the molecule has an internal plane of symmetry, so the rotation caused by one chiral center is exactly canceled by its mirror-image counterpart within the same molecule.

C. Are racemic mixtures: A racemic mixture is an equal mixture of two separate enantiomers (R and S) that cancels optical rotation externally. A meso compound is a single molecule that is optically inactive due to internal symmetry — it is not a mixture. This is an important distinction: racemic = two molecules averaging out; meso = one molecule canceling itself.

D. Contain only one chiral center: A molecule with only one chiral center cannot be a meso compound. Meso compounds require at least two chiral centers arranged so that an internal mirror plane makes one half the mirror image of the other, resulting in internal cancellation of optical rotation.

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: In the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog (CIP) system, substituent priority is determined by:
options: Molecular weight of the entire group
Atomic number of the atom directly bonded to the chiral center
Number of bonds in the substituent
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Atomic number of the atom directly bonded to the chiral center

In CIP priority rules, atoms directly attached to the chiral center are ranked by atomic number (higher atomic number = higher priority). If there is a tie, you move outward along the chain to the next atoms until a difference is found. For example: I > Br > Cl > S > O > N > C > H.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Molecular weight of the entire group: CIP rules look at atoms one at a time, starting with the atom directly attached to the chiral center. The molecular weight of the entire substituent group is irrelevant — only the atomic number at each point of comparison matters. For example, –CH₂CH₃ vs. –CH₂OH: you compare C vs. C first, then if tied, move outward to the next atoms.

C. Electronegativity of the substituent: Electronegativity describes how strongly an atom attracts bonding electrons, which influences polarity and reactivity. However, CIP priority is based on atomic number (number of protons), not electronegativity. While higher atomic number often correlates with higher electronegativity for light elements, this is not always true and is not the rule used.

D. Number of bonds in the substituent: The number of bonds in a substituent is not used to assign CIP priority. The system works by comparing atomic numbers at each successive point of difference, outward from the chiral center. Double and triple bonds are handled by the phantom atom rule: a C=O double bond is treated as if carbon is bonded to two oxygen atoms and oxygen to two carbon atoms.

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: A compound with the configuration (R)-2-bromobutane and its mirror image (S)-2-bromobutane are:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Enantiomers

Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images that differ in the configuration at every chiral center. (R)-2-bromobutane and (S)-2-bromobutane are mirror images with opposite configurations at the single stereocenter. They have identical physical properties except for the direction of optical rotation and their interaction with other chiral molecules.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Identical: They have identical physical properties except for the direction of optical rotation and their interaction with other chiral molecules.

C. Diastereomers: Diastereomers are stereoisomers that are not mirror images of each other. They occur when a molecule has two or more stereocenters and the configurations differ at some but not all centers. (R)- and (S)-2-bromobutane have only one chiral center, so their mirror-image relationship makes them enantiomers, not diastereomers.

D. Constitutional isomers: Constitutional (structural) isomers have the same molecular formula but different atom connectivity — for example, butan-1-ol and butan-2-ol. (R)- and (S)-2-bromobutane have identical connectivity; they differ only in the 3D arrangement of atoms around the chiral center, making them stereoisomers (specifically enantiomers).

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which statement about diastereomers is correct?
options: They are mirror images of each other
They are stereoisomers that are NOT mirror images
They always have opposite optical rotations
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. They are stereoisomers that are NOT mirror images

Diastereomers are stereoisomers that are not related as mirror images. They differ at some but not all stereocenters. Unlike enantiomers, diastereomers have different physical properties (melting point, boiling point, solubility, etc.) and can be separated by conventional techniques such as chromatography or recrystallization.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. They are mirror images of each other: Stereoisomers that are mirror images are called enantiomers, not diastereomers. Diastereomers are stereoisomers that are specifically NOT mirror images. For example, (2R,3R)-tartaric acid and (2R,3S)-tartaric acid (meso) are diastereomers because they differ at one stereocenter but not all.

B. They have identical physical properties: Unlike enantiomers (which share nearly all physical properties except optical rotation direction), diastereomers have different physical properties: different melting points, boiling points, solubilities, and densities. This makes them separable by ordinary techniques like crystallization or distillation.

D. They always have opposite optical rotations: Enantiomers always rotate plane-polarized light by equal amounts in opposite directions. Diastereomers have no such predictable relationship — their optical rotations can differ in both magnitude and sign. There is no rule requiring them to be opposite.

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The E/Z system for naming geometric isomers uses which priority rule?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Cahn-Ingold-Prelog priority rules

The E/Z system assigns priorities to the two substituents on each carbon of the double bond using CIP rules (based on atomic number). If the higher-priority groups are on the same side, the isomer is Z (zusammen, German for 'together'). If on opposite sides, it is E (entgegen, 'opposite'). This system is more general than cis/trans.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Alphabetical order of substituents: Alphabetical ordering is not used in any IUPAC stereochemical naming system. The E/Z system assigns priority based on CIP (Cahn-Ingold-Prelog) rules, which rank substituents by atomic number. If the two higher-priority groups are on the same side of the double bond, the configuration is Z (zusammen = together); if on opposite sides, it is E (entgegen = opposite).

C. Molecular weight of substituents: Molecular weight of substituents is not used in the E/Z system. CIP priority is determined by atomic number at each successive point of difference, not by mass. For instance, –Cl (atomic number 17) has higher priority than –CH₂CH₂CH₃ regardless of molecular weight.

D. Size of substituents: Physical size (van der Waals radius) of substituents has no role in the E/Z naming system. Priority is determined strictly by atomic number using CIP rules. This is a common misconception — a bulky tert-butyl group does not automatically get higher priority than a small bromine atom; Br (Z=35) outranks C (Z=6).

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: How many stereoisomers exist for 2,3-dibromobutane?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. 3

2,3-Dibromobutane has two chiral centers (C2 and C3). Normally, 2² = 4 stereoisomers would be expected. However, because the molecule has identical substituents on C2 and C3 (–CH₃ and –Br), one pair of configurations creates a meso compound (with an internal mirror plane). This reduces the total to 3 stereoisomers: (R,R), (S,S), and meso (R,S).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 2: Two chiral centers normally give 2² = 4 stereoisomers. However, 2,3-dibromobutane is a special case because the two halves of the molecule are identical. This creates a meso form (with an internal mirror plane) that reduces the count from 4 to 3: one (R,R), one (S,S), and one meso (R,S).

C. 4: The formula 2ⁿ = 2² = 4 gives the maximum possible stereoisomers for two chiral centers. However, because 2,3-dibromobutane has identical substituent patterns on C2 and C3, one pair of expected stereoisomers collapses into a single meso compound. This reduces the count from 4 to 3.

D. 5: Five stereoisomers is not possible from two chiral centers, since the maximum from 2ⁿ is 4, and the actual count can only decrease (due to meso forms), never increase beyond 2ⁿ. The correct answer is 3: a pair of enantiomers and one meso form.

Key Educational Points:

  • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms (chain, position, and functional group isomers).
  • Geometric (cis-trans) isomers differ in arrangement around a double bond or ring; they have different physical properties.
  • Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (optical isomers); they rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions and can have different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Glucose is classified as:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: D. An aldohexose

Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) has 6 carbon atoms (hexose) and an aldehyde functional group at C1 (aldo-). Therefore it is classified as an aldohexose. Fructose, by contrast, is a ketohexose (6 carbons with a ketone group at C2).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A ketopentose: A ketopentose is a 5-carbon sugar with a ketone (C=O) functional group. Ribulose is an example. Glucose has 6 carbons and an aldehyde group, so it is neither a pentose nor a ketose. Remember: “aldo-” = aldehyde at C1, “keto-” = ketone (usually at C2), “-pentose” = 5 carbons, “-hexose” = 6 carbons.

B. An aldopentose: An aldopentose is a 5-carbon sugar with an aldehyde group. Ribose (found in RNA) is a classic example. Glucose has the right functional group (aldehyde) but has 6 carbons, not 5, making it an aldohexose.

C. A ketohexose: Fructose, by contrast, is a ketohexose (6 carbons with a ketone group at c2).

Key Educational Points:

  • Carbohydrates are classified by size: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, maltose), and polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose).
  • Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides: alpha-linkages (digestible by humans, e.g., starch) vs. beta-linkages (indigestible, e.g., cellulose/fiber).
  • Glucose (C6H12O6) is the primary energy currency of cells, metabolized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The α and β anomers of D-glucose differ in the configuration at:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. C-1 (the anomeric carbon)

When glucose cyclizes to form the pyranose ring, the C1 aldehyde carbon becomes a new stereocenter called the anomeric carbon. The α-anomer has the –OH at C1 axial (trans to the CH₂OH group in the Haworth projection), while the β-anomer has it equatorial (cis to CH₂OH). This difference underlies mutarotation.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. C-2: C-2 is the carbon whose configuration distinguishes epimers like glucose and mannose (they differ only at C-2). The anomeric carbon in glucose is C-1, which is the carbon that forms a new stereocenter when the open-chain form cyclizes into the ring form.

B. C-4: C-4 configuration distinguishes glucose from galactose (they are C-4 epimers). Anomers specifically differ at the anomeric carbon (C-1 in aldoses like glucose), which is the hemiacetal carbon formed during ring closure.

D. C-6: C-6 is the terminal carbon bearing a –CH₂OH group in glucose; it is not a stereocenter in the ring form and plays no role in anomer formation. Anomers arise from the two possible orientations of the –OH group at C-1 when the open chain cyclizes: axial (α) or equatorial (β) in the chair conformation.

Key Educational Points:

  • Carbohydrates are classified by size: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, maltose), and polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose).
  • Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides: alpha-linkages (digestible by humans, e.g., starch) vs. beta-linkages (indigestible, e.g., cellulose/fiber).
  • Glucose (C6H12O6) is the primary energy currency of cells, metabolized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Cellulose differs from starch primarily in:
options: The type of glycosidic linkage (α vs. β)
The number of carbon atoms per unit
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. The type of glycosidic linkage (α vs. β)

Both cellulose and starch are polymers of D-glucose, but starch uses α-1,4 glycosidic bonds (and α-1,6 for branches), while cellulose uses β-1,4 glycosidic bonds. This seemingly small difference has huge consequences: humans can digest starch (via α-amylase) but cannot digest cellulose because we lack the enzyme cellulase to cleave β-linkages.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. The monosaccharide units used: Both cellulose and starch are polymers of the same monosaccharide: D-glucose. The difference lies in how the glucose units are connected. Starch uses α(1→4) glycosidic bonds (easily digested by human amylase), while cellulose uses β(1→4) bonds (humans lack cellulase to break these, which is why we cannot digest cellulose/fiber).

C. The number of carbon atoms per unit: Both cellulose and starch are built from glucose, which has 6 carbon atoms per unit. The critical difference is the stereochemistry of the glycosidic bond: α in starch versus β in cellulose. This small geometric difference has enormous biological consequences for digestibility and structural properties.

D. The molecular weight: While cellulose molecules tend to be longer than individual amylose or amylopectin chains, molecular weight is not the primary distinguishing feature. The fundamental difference is the type of glycosidic linkage (α vs. β), which determines the polymer’s shape — starch coils into helices, while cellulose forms straight, rigid fibers that hydrogen-bond into tough sheets.

Key Educational Points:

  • Carbohydrates are classified by size: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, maltose), and polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose).
  • Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides: alpha-linkages (digestible by humans, e.g., starch) vs. beta-linkages (indigestible, e.g., cellulose/fiber).
  • Glucose (C6H12O6) is the primary energy currency of cells, metabolized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Lactose is a reducing sugar because:
options: It has a free anomeric carbon on the galactose unit
It has a free anomeric carbon on the glucose unit
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. It has a free anomeric carbon on the glucose unit

Lactose consists of β-D-galactose linked β-1,4 to D-glucose. The glycosidic bond uses the C1 anomeric carbon of galactose, but the C1 of glucose remains free. This free anomeric carbon can open to form an aldehyde, which can reduce Tollens' or Fehling's reagent, making lactose a reducing sugar.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. It contains fructose: Lactose contains galactose and glucose, not fructose. Sucrose is the disaccharide that contains fructose (bonded to glucose). In lactose, galactose is linked through its C-1 anomeric carbon to C-4 of glucose, leaving glucose’s C-1 anomeric carbon free to open and act as the reducing end.

B. It has a free anomeric carbon on the galactose unit: In lactose, the galactose unit’s anomeric carbon (C-1) is involved in the β(1→4) glycosidic bond to glucose, so it is NOT free. It is the glucose unit that retains a free anomeric carbon capable of ring-opening to expose its aldehyde, which can then reduce Benedict’s or Fehling’s reagent.

D. Both anomeric carbons are free: If both anomeric carbons were free, the two monosaccharides would not be bonded together at all (since the glycosidic bond requires at least one anomeric carbon). In lactose, galactose’s anomeric C-1 forms the glycosidic linkage, while glucose’s anomeric C-1 remains free. Compare this to sucrose, where both anomeric carbons participate in the bond, making sucrose non-reducing.

Key Educational Points:

  • Carbohydrates are classified by size: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, maltose), and polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose).
  • Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides: alpha-linkages (digestible by humans, e.g., starch) vs. beta-linkages (indigestible, e.g., cellulose/fiber).
  • Glucose (C6H12O6) is the primary energy currency of cells, metabolized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: D-glucose and D-mannose are:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Epimers differing at C-2

Epimers are diastereomers that differ in configuration at only one stereocenter. D-glucose and D-mannose have the same configuration at C3, C4, and C5, but opposite configuration at C2. D-glucose and D-galactose are C-4 epimers. Enantiomers would differ at every stereocenter (D-glucose vs. L-glucose).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Enantiomers: Enantiomers would differ at every stereocenter (d-glucose vs.

C. Anomers: Anomers are cyclic sugar forms that differ specifically at the anomeric carbon (C-1 in aldoses) — the α and β forms of the same sugar. D-glucose and D-mannose differ at C-2 in their open-chain forms, so they are epimers, not anomers.

D. Identical compounds: D-glucose and D-mannose have the same molecular formula (C₆H₁₂O₆) but different configurations at C-2. They are clearly different compounds with distinct physical properties (different melting points, specific rotations). The term for sugars differing at exactly one stereocenter is epimers.

Key Educational Points:

  • Carbohydrates are classified by size: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, maltose), and polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose).
  • Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides: alpha-linkages (digestible by humans, e.g., starch) vs. beta-linkages (indigestible, e.g., cellulose/fiber).
  • Glucose (C6H12O6) is the primary energy currency of cells, metabolized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Mutarotation is the process by which:
options: The optical rotation of a freshly dissolved sugar changes over time until equilibrium between anomers is reached
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. The optical rotation of a freshly dissolved sugar changes over time until equilibrium between anomers is reached

When a pure anomer of glucose (either α or β) is dissolved in water, the ring can open and reclose to form both anomers. This gradual interconversion causes the specific rotation to change over time until an equilibrium mixture is reached (approximately 36% α and 64% β for D-glucose at 25°C).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A sugar undergoes hydrolysis: Hydrolysis is the cleavage of a bond by addition of water, as when a disaccharide is split into monosaccharides. Mutarotation does not break any covalent bonds between sugar units — it is the interconversion between α and β anomeric forms of the same monosaccharide through the open-chain intermediate.

C. A sugar loses water: Losing water describes a dehydration or condensation reaction (like forming a glycosidic bond). Mutarotation involves the ring opening and reclosing of a single sugar molecule in aqueous solution, allowing the α and β anomers to equilibrate. The observed optical rotation gradually changes until the equilibrium mixture is reached.

D. A polysaccharide breaks down: Polysaccharide breakdown (hydrolysis of glycosidic bonds by enzymes like amylase) is a completely different process. Mutarotation refers specifically to the spontaneous change in optical rotation observed when a pure anomer of a sugar is dissolved in water, as it equilibrates between its α and β forms.

Key Educational Points:

  • Carbohydrates are classified by size: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, maltose), and polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose).
  • Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides: alpha-linkages (digestible by humans, e.g., starch) vs. beta-linkages (indigestible, e.g., cellulose/fiber).
  • Glucose (C6H12O6) is the primary energy currency of cells, metabolized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which test can detect reducing sugars?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Benedict's test

Benedict's reagent contains Cu²⁺ ions (blue) in alkaline citrate solution. Reducing sugars (which have a free or potentially free anomeric carbon) reduce Cu²⁺ to Cu₂O (brick-red precipitate). The Biuret test detects proteins, the ninhydrin test detects amino acids, and the xanthoproteic test detects aromatic amino acids.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Biuret test: The biuret test detects proteins (peptide bonds), not sugars. It uses Cu²⁺ ions in strongly alkaline NaOH solution; when peptide bonds are present, they form violet-colored complexes with the copper. A minimum of two peptide bonds (i.e., a tripeptide or larger) is required for a positive result.

C. Ninhydrin test: The ninhydrin test detects free amino acids and primary amines. Ninhydrin reacts with the α-amino group to produce a purple-blue color known as Ruhemann’s purple. It is widely used in forensics (fingerprint detection) and amino acid analysis after chromatography.

D. Xanthoproteic test: The xanthoproteic test specifically detects aromatic amino acids (tyrosine, tryptophan, phenylalanine) in proteins. Concentrated nitric acid nitrates the aromatic rings, producing a yellow color that turns orange upon addition of base. It does not react with sugars.

Key Educational Points:

  • Carbohydrates are classified by size: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, maltose), and polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose).
  • Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides: alpha-linkages (digestible by humans, e.g., starch) vs. beta-linkages (indigestible, e.g., cellulose/fiber).
  • Glucose (C6H12O6) is the primary energy currency of cells, metabolized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Fructose adopts predominantly which ring form in solution?
options: Both pyranose and furanose in equal amounts
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: A. Pyranose (6-membered ring)

Free fructose in aqueous solution exists predominantly (~68-75%) as beta-D-fructopyranose, a six-membered (pyranose) ring. Although fructose is often associated with its furanose form (as found in sucrose), when dissolved in water as the free monosaccharide, the thermodynamically more stable pyranose form predominates. The pyranose ring is formed by internal hemiketal formation between the C-2 keto group and the C-6 hydroxyl group.
