D. Dehydration: Dehydration (loss of water) converts an alcohol to an alkene, not to a carboxylic acid. For example, heating ethanol with concentrated H₂SO₄ produces ethylene. The conversion to a carboxylic acid requires oxidation (adding oxygen bonds), typically proceeding through an aldehyde intermediate.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Conversion of a carboxylic acid to an acid chloride is best accomplished using:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: C. SOCl₂ (thionyl chloride)

Thionyl chloride (SOCl₂) reacts with carboxylic acids to give acid chlorides, SO₂, and HCl — both gaseous by-products that leave the reaction mixture, driving the reaction to completion: RCOOH + SOCl₂ → RCOCl + SO₂↑ + HCl↑. PCl₃ and PCl₅ can also be used but produce less convenient by-products.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. HCl: HCl is a simple acid that cannot convert a carboxylic acid to an acid chloride — the reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable. SOCl₂ (thionyl chloride) drives the reaction forward because its byproducts (SO₂ and HCl) are gases that escape the reaction, shifting equilibrium toward products.

B. NaCl: NaCl is a stable ionic salt that cannot donate chloride in a way that displaces –OH from a carboxylic acid. Acid chloride synthesis requires an activating reagent like SOCl₂, PCl₃, or PCl₅ that makes the hydroxyl a better leaving group while providing reactive chloride.

D. Cl₂/UV light: Cl₂/UV light causes free radical halogenation of C–H bonds (as in the chlorination of alkanes), not conversion of –COOH to –COCl. Acid chloride formation requires nucleophilic acyl substitution with reagents like thionyl chloride (SOCl₂), which also conveniently produces gaseous byproducts.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which functional group transformation occurs when an alkene reacts with H₂ over a Pd/C catalyst?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Reduction to an alkane

Catalytic hydrogenation (H₂/Pd, Pt, or Ni) adds H₂ across the double bond in a syn fashion (both H atoms add to the same face), converting the alkene to an alkane. This is a heterogeneous catalytic reaction where both H₂ and the alkene adsorb onto the metal surface before H atoms are delivered.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Oxidation to an epoxide: Epoxide formation from an alkene requires a peracid (like mCPBA) or a peroxide, not H₂ and Pd/C. Catalytic hydrogenation (H₂/Pd) adds two hydrogen atoms across the double bond, reducing the alkene to an alkane in a syn addition.

C. Isomerization to a cycloalkane: Isomerization to a cycloalkane would require forming a new C–C bond to close a ring, which catalytic hydrogenation does not do. H₂/Pd simply adds hydrogen atoms across the π bond of the alkene. The carbon skeleton remains unchanged — only the degree of saturation changes.

D. Halogenation: Halogenation (adding Br₂ or Cl₂) is a completely different reaction from hydrogenation. H₂/Pd is a classic heterogeneous catalytic hydrogenation: the alkene and H₂ adsorb onto the palladium surface, and both hydrogen atoms add to the same face of the double bond (syn addition), producing an alkane.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: A Wittig reaction converts a ketone or aldehyde into:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. An alkene

The Wittig reaction uses a phosphonium ylide (R₃P=CR'₂) to react with an aldehyde or ketone, replacing the C=O with a C=C double bond. The by-product is triphenylphosphine oxide (Ph₃P=O). This is a powerful method for synthesizing alkenes with specific placement of the double bond, unlike elimination reactions which may give mixtures.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. An alcohol: Reduction of a ketone or aldehyde (using NaBH₄ or LiAlH₄) produces an alcohol. The Wittig reaction does something different: a phosphorus ylide (R₃P=CHR’) reacts with the carbonyl to replace C=O with C=C, producing an alkene and triphenylphosphine oxide as a byproduct.

C. An amine: Amines are produced by reductive amination of ketones/aldehydes (reaction with an amine followed by reduction). The Wittig reaction specifically converts a C=O to a C=C bond. It is one of the most important methods for making alkenes with precise control over double bond placement.

D. An ester: Ester formation from a carbonyl requires an oxidation step (like Baeyer–Villiger oxidation for ketones). The Wittig reaction replaces the carbonyl oxygen with a carbon from the phosphorus ylide, forming a new C=C double bond. The driving force is the formation of the very strong P=O bond in the triphenylphosphine oxide byproduct.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Beckmann rearrangement of an oxime produces:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: A. An amide

The Beckmann rearrangement is an acid-catalyzed conversion of an oxime (R₁R₂C=NOH) to an amide. The group anti to the hydroxyl migrates from carbon to nitrogen as the N–O bond breaks. This rearrangement is industrially important for the production of caprolactam (from cyclohexanone oxime), which is the monomer for nylon-6.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. A nitrile: Nitriles (R–C≡N) can be formed by dehydration of primary amides or aldoximes, but this is not the Beckmann rearrangement. The Beckmann rearrangement specifically converts a ketoxime to a substituted amide through migration of the group anti to the hydroxyl, with ring expansion if the oxime is cyclic.

C. An amine: Amines (R–NH₂) are formed by reduction of amides, nitriles, or imines. The Beckmann rearrangement produces an amide, not an amine. The mechanism involves protonation of the oxime’s hydroxyl, departure of water, 1,2-shift of the anti-periplanar group to nitrogen, and hydrolysis to the amide.

D. An imine: An imine (Schiff base, R₂C=NR) is actually the starting point: an oxime is a special type of imine (where N is bonded to –OH). The Beckmann rearrangement transforms this oxime into an amide. Industrially, this reaction is used to convert cyclohexanone oxime to caprolactam, the monomer for nylon-6.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Protection of a hydroxyl group in organic synthesis is commonly achieved by converting it to:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. A silyl ether (e.g., TBS ether)

Silyl ethers (such as TBS — tert-butyldimethylsilyl ether, or TMS — trimethylsilyl ether) are widely used protecting groups for alcohols. They are stable under many reaction conditions but can be selectively removed with fluoride ions (e.g., TBAF). Protection/deprotection strategies are essential in multi-step synthesis when a functional group must survive reactions intended for other parts of the molecule.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A thiol (–SH): A thiol (–SH) is the sulfur analog of an alcohol. While thiol-for-hydroxyl swaps exist in organic synthesis, this is a functional group transformation, not a protecting group strategy. Protection means temporarily masking a group so it can be restored later. Silyl ethers (like TBS, TMS, TIPS) are ideal because they are easily added and cleanly removed under mild conditions.

C. An amine (–NH₂): An amine (–NH₂) is not used to protect a hydroxyl group. Converting –OH to –NH₂ would be a major functional group change, not protection. Good protecting groups are easily installed, stable during subsequent reactions, and readily removed when needed. Silyl ethers fit all three criteria perfectly.

D. A sulfonate ester: A sulfonate ester (like tosylate or mesylate) converts an –OH into an excellent leaving group for substitution or elimination, which is the opposite of protection. A protecting group must preserve the functional group for later use. Silyl ethers protect the –OH by making it unreactive while allowing selective chemistry on other parts of the molecule.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: In retrosynthetic analysis, the symbol ⇒ represents:
options: A disconnection (working backward from product to simpler precursors)
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. A disconnection (working backward from product to simpler precursors)

Retrosynthetic analysis, developed by E.J. Corey, works backward from the target molecule to available starting materials. The retrosynthetic arrow (⇒) indicates a transform — the mental operation of breaking the target into simpler precursors (synthons). This strategic approach identifies the most efficient synthetic routes by recognizing key disconnections and functional group interconversions.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. A forward reaction step: A forward reaction step is shown by a regular reaction arrow (→). The retrosynthetic arrow (⇒) indicates the reverse thought process: working backward from the target molecule to identify simpler precursors. Retrosynthesis is a planning strategy, not an actual reaction — it maps out how a complex molecule can be built from available starting materials.

C. An equilibrium: An equilibrium is represented by the double-headed arrow (⇌), indicating a reversible reaction. The retrosynthetic arrow (⇒) has a different meaning entirely: it indicates a conceptual disconnection where you mentally break a bond in the target to reveal potential precursor molecules (synthons).

D. A catalytic step: A catalytic step has no special arrow notation; it is simply shown as a reaction arrow with the catalyst written above or below. The retrosynthetic arrow (⇒) means “can be derived from” — pointing from a complex target molecule to simpler building blocks. This approach, pioneered by E. J. Corey, earned him the 1990 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Swern oxidation uses which reagent system to oxidize an alcohol to a carbonyl compound?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. DMSO/oxalyl chloride/base

The Swern oxidation uses DMSO activated by oxalyl chloride ((COCl)₂) at low temperature (−78°C), followed by an amine base (usually triethylamine). It oxidizes primary alcohols to aldehydes and secondary alcohols to ketones under mild, chromium-free conditions. The mild conditions and absence of toxic heavy metals make it advantageous for sensitive substrates.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. CrO₃/pyridine: CrO₃/pyridine is the Collins reagent, a chromium-based oxidant. It works but produces toxic chromium waste. The Swern oxidation is preferred for sensitive substrates because it uses DMSO activated by oxalyl chloride at −78°C, followed by base (triethylamine), and avoids heavy metal reagents entirely.

C. PCC/CH₂Cl₂: PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) in CH₂Cl₂ is a mild chromium-based oxidant that converts primary alcohols to aldehydes without over-oxidation to carboxylic acids. The Swern oxidation achieves the same selectivity using DMSO/oxalyl chloride/base instead — a gentler, metal-free approach especially suited for complex, acid-sensitive substrates.

D. Jones reagent: Jones reagent (CrO₃ in aqueous H₂SO₄/acetone) is a strong oxidant that converts primary alcohols all the way to carboxylic acids. The Swern oxidation, by contrast, stops at the aldehyde stage and uses no metals. It works by activating DMSO to form a reactive intermediate that oxidizes the alcohol while itself being reduced to dimethyl sulfide (DMS).

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: An amide can be converted to an amine with one fewer carbon atom by which named reaction?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: B. Hofmann rearrangement

The Hofmann rearrangement (or Hofmann degradation) treats a primary amide (RCONH₂) with bromine and base (Br₂/NaOH) to give a primary amine (RNH₂) with one fewer carbon (the carbonyl carbon is lost as CO₂). The mechanism involves formation of an isocyanate intermediate followed by hydrolysis. The Curtius rearrangement uses acyl azides to achieve a similar result.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Curtius rearrangement: The curtius rearrangement uses acyl azides to achieve a similar result.

C. Lossen rearrangement: The Lossen rearrangement converts a hydroxamic acid derivative to an isocyanate, ultimately yielding an amine with one fewer carbon. It is mechanistically related to the Hofmann and Curtius rearrangements (all involve nitrene or isocyanate intermediates), but it starts from a different substrate (activated hydroxamate, not a simple amide).

D. Schmidt reaction: The Schmidt reaction uses hydrazoic acid (HN₃) with a carboxylic acid (not an amide) in the presence of a strong acid catalyst to produce an amine with one fewer carbon. All four reactions (Hofmann, Curtius, Lossen, Schmidt) achieve the same net transformation but start from different substrates and use different reagents.

Key Educational Points:

  • Chemical structure determines chemical properties — understanding structure allows prediction of reactivity and behavior.
  • Conservation laws (mass, energy, charge) govern all chemical reactions and must be satisfied in balanced equations.
  • Thermodynamics tells us whether a reaction CAN occur (spontaneity); kinetics tells us how FAST it occurs.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which statement best describes how enzymes accelerate chemical reactions?
options: They increase the temperature of the reaction
They lower the activation energy of the reaction
They shift the equilibrium toward products
They increase the free energy change (ΔG) of the reaction
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: They lower the activation energy of the reaction

Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up reactions by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy barrier. They achieve this by stabilizing the transition state through precise molecular interactions in the active site — including hydrogen bonds, ionic interactions, and transient covalent bonds with the substrate.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Increase temperature: Enzymes do not change temperature; they work by lowering the energy barrier at the existing temperature, which is why biological reactions proceed rapidly at body temperature.

C. Shift equilibrium: Enzymes accelerate both forward and reverse reactions equally — they do not change the position of equilibrium or the equilibrium constant (Keq).

D. Increase ΔG: Enzymes do not alter the thermodynamics (ΔG) of a reaction. They only affect kinetics (how fast equilibrium is reached), not whether or which direction is favorable.

Bottom Line: Enzymes lower activation energy without changing ΔG or equilibrium. They are specific (lock-and-key or induced-fit model), reusable, and regulated by factors like pH, temperature, inhibitors, and allosteric modulators.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: An enzyme that catalyzes the transfer of a phosphate group from ATP to a substrate is classified as a:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Transferase

The International Union of Biochemistry classifies enzymes into six major classes based on the type of reaction they catalyze. Transferases catalyze the transfer of functional groups (such as methyl, amino, phosphate, or glycosyl groups) from one molecule to another. Kinases are a specific subclass of transferases that transfer phosphate groups from ATP to substrates.

ClassReaction TypeExample
OxidoreductaseElectron transfer (redox)Dehydrogenases, oxidases
TransferaseGroup transferKinases, transaminases
HydrolaseHydrolysis (bond cleavage by water)Lipase, protease
LyaseNon-hydrolytic bond cleavageDecarboxylase, aldolase
IsomeraseStructural rearrangementMutase, racemase
LigaseBond formation using ATPDNA ligase, synthetases

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Oxidoreductase: These catalyze oxidation-reduction reactions (electron transfer), not group transfer.

C. Hydrolase: Hydrolases break bonds by adding water; they do not transfer groups between molecules.

D. Lyase: Lyases cleave bonds without hydrolysis or oxidation (e.g., removing CO₂ or H₂O), not transferring groups.

Bottom Line: The six enzyme classes are: Oxidoreductases, Transferases, Hydrolases, Lyases, Isomerases, and Ligases. Kinases (phosphate transferases) are among the most important transferases in cell signaling and metabolism.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which vitamin serves as the precursor for the coenzymes NAD⁺ and NADP⁺, and its deficiency causes pellagra (the disease of the 4 D's: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, death)?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Niacin (B3)

Niacin (nicotinic acid or nicotinamide) is the precursor of NAD⁺ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) and NADP⁺, which are essential coenzymes in hundreds of redox reactions in metabolism. NAD⁺ is crucial in glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation. Severe deficiency leads to pellagra, classically characterized by dermatitis (on sun-exposed skin), diarrhea, dementia, and if untreated, death.

VitaminCoenzyme FormDeficiency Disease
Thiamine (B1)TPP (thiamine pyrophosphate)Beriberi, Wernicke-Korsakoff
Riboflavin (B2)FAD, FMNCheilosis, angular stomatitis
Niacin (B3)NAD⁺, NADP⁺Pellagra (4 D's)
Pyridoxine (B6)PLP (pyridoxal phosphate)Peripheral neuropathy, anemia

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Thiamine (B1): Forms TPP, a coenzyme for pyruvate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase; deficiency causes beriberi (wet: cardiac; dry: neurological).

B. Riboflavin (B2): Forms FAD and FMN, electron carriers in the ETC and various oxidases; deficiency causes oral/skin lesions, not pellagra.

D. Pyridoxine (B6): Forms PLP, essential for transamination and amino acid metabolism; deficiency causes neuropathy and sideroblastic anemia.

Bottom Line: Niacin → NAD⁺/NADP⁺ → essential for cellular redox reactions. Pellagra = 4 D's. Niacin can be synthesized from tryptophan (60 mg tryptophan → 1 mg niacin), so corn-based diets (low tryptophan) increase pellagra risk.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle) takes place in which cellular compartment?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Mitochondrial matrix

The citric acid cycle (also called the Krebs cycle or TCA cycle) occurs in the mitochondrial matrix, the innermost compartment of the mitochondrion. Here, acetyl-CoA (derived from pyruvate, fatty acids, or amino acids) is oxidized through a series of eight enzymatic reactions, generating CO₂, NADH, FADH₂, and GTP. The reduced coenzymes then donate electrons to the electron transport chain in the inner mitochondrial membrane.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Cytoplasm: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm; the citric acid cycle requires transport of pyruvate into the mitochondria first.

C. Inner mitochondrial membrane: This is where the electron transport chain and oxidative phosphorylation occur, not the citric acid cycle itself.

D. Endoplasmic reticulum: The ER is involved in protein folding (rough ER) and lipid synthesis (smooth ER), not in the citric acid cycle.

Bottom Line: Per acetyl-CoA, the Krebs cycle produces 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP, and 2 CO₂. Since one glucose yields 2 acetyl-CoA, the cycle turns twice per glucose molecule. Location: mitochondrial matrix.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The majority of ATP produced during aerobic respiration is generated by:
options: Substrate-level phosphorylation in the Krebs cycle
Oxidative phosphorylation (chemiosmosis) in the electron transport chain
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Oxidative phosphorylation (chemiosmosis)

Oxidative phosphorylation produces approximately 30-34 of the ~36-38 total ATP molecules generated per glucose during aerobic respiration. Electrons from NADH and FADH₂ pass through protein complexes (I-IV) in the inner mitochondrial membrane, pumping protons (H⁺) into the intermembrane space. The resulting proton gradient drives ATP synthase (Complex V) to phosphorylate ADP to ATP — this coupling of electron transport to ATP synthesis is called chemiosmosis.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis: Glycolysis produces only 2 net ATP per glucose via substrate-level phosphorylation — a small fraction of total yield.

B. Substrate-level phosphorylation in Krebs cycle: The Krebs cycle produces 2 GTP (equivalent to 2 ATP) per glucose via substrate-level phosphorylation.

D. Photophosphorylation: This occurs in chloroplasts during photosynthesis, not during cellular respiration in mitochondria.

Bottom Line: ~90% of ATP from aerobic respiration comes from oxidative phosphorylation. NADH yields ~2.5 ATP; FADH₂ yields ~1.5 ATP (enters ETC at Complex II, bypassing Complex I). Peter Mitchell received the Nobel Prize for the chemiosmotic hypothesis.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which nitrogenous base is found in RNA but NOT in DNA?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Uracil

RNA contains the bases adenine, guanine, cytosine, and uracil. DNA contains adenine, guanine, cytosine, and thymine instead of uracil. Uracil differs from thymine by lacking a methyl group at the C-5 position. In RNA, uracil pairs with adenine through two hydrogen bonds, just as thymine does in DNA.

FeatureDNARNA
SugarDeoxyriboseRibose
BasesA, T, G, CA, U, G, C
StructureDouble-stranded helixUsually single-stranded
StabilityMore stableLess stable (2'-OH reactive)

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Adenine: Found in both DNA and RNA; it pairs with thymine in DNA and uracil in RNA.

B. Guanine: Found in both DNA and RNA; it pairs with cytosine in both nucleic acids.

D. Cytosine: Found in both DNA and RNA; it pairs with guanine through three hydrogen bonds in both.

Bottom Line: DNA uses thymine; RNA uses uracil. The methyl group on thymine helps DNA repair enzymes distinguish thymine from deaminated cytosine (which produces uracil), protecting DNA integrity — a likely evolutionary reason DNA uses thymine.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which type of lipid is the main structural component of biological cell membranes?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Phospholipids

Phospholipids are amphipathic molecules with a hydrophilic phosphate head group and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails. In aqueous environments, they spontaneously arrange into a bilayer — the fundamental architecture of all biological membranes. This bilayer acts as a selectively permeable barrier, allowing small nonpolar molecules to pass while restricting ions and large polar molecules.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Triglycerides: Triglycerides (triacylglycerols) are energy storage lipids found in adipose tissue; they are fully hydrophobic and do not form bilayers.

C. Cholesterol: Cholesterol is an important membrane component that modulates fluidity, but it is not the primary structural lipid — it inserts between phospholipids.

D. Waxes: Waxes are esters of long-chain fatty acids and alcohols; they serve as waterproof coatings on surfaces (leaves, feathers) rather than membrane components.

Bottom Line: Phospholipid bilayers form spontaneously due to the hydrophobic effect. The fluid mosaic model describes membranes as dynamic structures with phospholipids, cholesterol, and proteins that can move laterally. Membrane fluidity is influenced by fatty acid saturation, chain length, and cholesterol content.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: At physiological pH (~7.4), a free amino acid exists primarily as a:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Zwitterion (NH₃⁺-CHR-COO⁻)

At physiological pH, which is near the isoelectric point of most amino acids, the amino group is protonated (NH₃⁺, acting as a weak acid) and the carboxyl group is deprotonated (COO⁻, acting as a conjugate base). This creates a dipolar ion called a zwitterion — the molecule carries both positive and negative charges but has zero net charge. This explains why amino acids have high melting points, are soluble in water, and migrate in an electric field depending on pH.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Fully protonated cation: This form exists only at very low pH (strongly acidic conditions) when both groups are protonated.

B. Fully deprotonated anion: This form exists only at very high pH (strongly basic conditions) when both groups are deprotonated.

D. Uncharged molecule: The uncharged form (both groups in their non-ionized state) does not exist in significant amounts at any pH in aqueous solution.

Bottom Line: At physiological pH, amino acids are zwitterions. The isoelectric point (pI) is the pH at which the amino acid has zero net charge. Amino acids with ionizable R-groups (Asp, Glu, Lys, Arg, His) have more complex titration curves.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The bond that links amino acids together in a polypeptide chain is called a:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Peptide bond

The peptide bond is a covalent amide bond formed by a condensation reaction between the carboxyl group (-COOH) of one amino acid and the amino group (-NH₂) of the next, with the loss of one water molecule. The peptide bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance, making it planar and rigid — this constrains protein backbone geometry and influences folding.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Glycosidic bond: Links monosaccharides together in carbohydrates (e.g., glucose units in starch); not found between amino acids.

B. Ester bond: Links fatty acids to glycerol in lipids (triglycerides, phospholipids); also found in some other biomolecules but not in peptide chains.

D. Phosphodiester bond: Links nucleotides together in nucleic acids (DNA and RNA backbone); not found in polypeptides.

Bottom Line: Peptide bonds link amino acids in proteins. Glycosidic bonds link sugars. Phosphodiester bonds link nucleotides. Ester bonds link fatty acids to glycerol. Each biopolymer has its characteristic linkage type.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: According to Le Chatelier's principle, if the concentration of a reactant is increased in a system at equilibrium, the system will:
options: Shift toward products to partially consume the added reactant
Shift toward reactants to produce more of the added substance
Remain unchanged because equilibrium is already established
Reach a new equilibrium with a different equilibrium constant
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Shift toward products

Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium is subjected to a stress, it shifts in the direction that partially relieves that stress. Adding more reactant increases the reactant concentration, so the system shifts toward products (forward reaction accelerates) until a new equilibrium is established at the same Keq but with different concentrations.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. Shift toward reactants: This would increase the stress rather than relieve it — the system shifts in the opposite direction of the perturbation.

C. Remain unchanged: The system will always respond to a concentration change by shifting to counteract it; only a catalyst leaves the equilibrium position unchanged.

D. New equilibrium constant: The equilibrium constant (Keq) depends only on temperature. Changing concentration shifts the position of equilibrium but does not change Keq.

Bottom Line: Le Chatelier's principle: add reactant → shift right (more products). Add product → shift left (more reactants). Only temperature changes alter Keq itself. Catalysts speed up attainment of equilibrium but do not change its position.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: A buffer solution is prepared by mixing a weak acid (HA) with its conjugate base (A⁻). What is the pH when [HA] = [A⁻]?
options: pH = pKa of the weak acid
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: pH = pKa

The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation (pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA])) relates buffer pH to the ratio of conjugate base to weak acid. When the concentrations of HA and A⁻ are equal, log([A⁻]/[HA]) = log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa. This is also the point of maximum buffer capacity — the solution is most resistant to pH changes when pH equals pKa.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. pH = 0: pH = 0 corresponds to 1 M strong acid (like HCl); it has no relation to the buffer's pKa.

B. pH = 7.0: pH 7 is neutral, but buffers can have any pH depending on the acid chosen. Only if pKa happens to be 7 would this be correct.

D. pH = 14 - pKa: This expression has no chemical meaning in the Henderson-Hasselbalch context. (14 - pKa would give pKb of the conjugate base, which relates to pOH, not pH directly.)

Bottom Line: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). When [A⁻] = [HA], pH = pKa. Effective buffers work within ±1 pH unit of pKa. The bicarbonate buffer system (pKa ≈ 6.1) is the primary blood buffer, maintaining pH 7.35-7.45.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: In a galvanic (voltaic) cell, oxidation occurs at which electrode?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Anode

In any electrochemical cell (galvanic or electrolytic), oxidation always occurs at the anode and reduction always occurs at the cathode. In a galvanic cell, the anode is the negative terminal (electrons flow from anode through the external circuit to cathode), and the spontaneous redox reaction generates electrical energy.

FeatureAnodeCathode
ReactionOxidation (electron loss)Reduction (electron gain)
Sign (galvanic)Negative (−)Positive (+)
Sign (electrolytic)Positive (+)Negative (−)
MnemonicAn OxRed Cat

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Cathode: Reduction occurs at the cathode (cations gain electrons here). Remember: Red Cat = Reduction at Cathode.

C. Salt bridge: The salt bridge provides ion flow to maintain electrical neutrality in each half-cell; no redox reactions occur in it.

D. Both electrodes equally: Oxidation and reduction are separate half-reactions occurring at different electrodes, not at the same one.

Bottom Line: An Ox Red Cat = Anode Oxidation, Reduction Cathode. In galvanic cells, the reaction is spontaneous (E°cell > 0, ΔG < 0). In electrolytic cells, external voltage drives a non-spontaneous reaction.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: How many moles of water are produced when 2 moles of hydrogen gas react completely with 1 mole of oxygen gas? (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O)
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: 2 moles

The balanced equation 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O shows that the mole ratio of hydrogen to oxygen to water is 2:1:2. When 2 moles of H₂ react with 1 mole of O₂, exactly 2 moles of water are produced. The coefficients in a balanced equation directly represent the mole ratios of reactants and products.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 1 mole: This would imply a 2:1:1 ratio, which does not satisfy conservation of hydrogen atoms (4 H atoms in reactants must appear in products).

C. 3 moles: This would require 6 hydrogen atoms and 3 oxygen atoms in the products, which does not match the 4 H and 2 O atoms provided by the reactants.

D. 4 moles: This would require 4 oxygen atoms in the products, but only 2 are available from 1 mole of O₂.

Bottom Line: Balanced equations give mole ratios. The law of conservation of mass requires that atoms are neither created nor destroyed. Stoichiometry calculations: convert given → moles → use mole ratio → convert to desired unit.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: A solution is prepared by dissolving 58.5 g of NaCl (molar mass = 58.5 g/mol) in enough water to make 1.00 L of solution. What is the molarity of this solution?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: 1.00 M

Molarity (M) is defined as moles of solute per liter of solution. First, calculate moles: 58.5 g ÷ 58.5 g/mol = 1.00 mol. Then, molarity = 1.00 mol / 1.00 L = 1.00 M. This is one of the most fundamental calculations in chemistry.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 0.50 M: This would result from dissolving only 29.25 g of NaCl in 1 L, or 58.5 g in 2 L.

C. 2.00 M: This would require 117 g of NaCl in 1 L, or 58.5 g in 0.5 L.

D. 58.5 M: This confuses grams with molarity; molarity requires conversion to moles first.

Bottom Line: Molarity = moles of solute / liters of solution. Always convert mass to moles using molar mass before calculating molarity. Other concentration units include molality (mol/kg solvent), mass percent, and mole fraction.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: According to the ideal gas law (PV = nRT), if the temperature of a fixed amount of gas is doubled (in Kelvin) while volume is held constant, the pressure will:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Double

From PV = nRT, when n and V are constant, P is directly proportional to T (in Kelvin): P = nRT/V. Doubling the absolute temperature doubles the pressure. This relationship is known as Gay-Lussac's law and reflects the fact that gas molecules move faster at higher temperatures, colliding with container walls more frequently and forcefully.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Halve: Pressure would halve if temperature were halved (at constant V), not doubled.

B. Remain the same: Pressure remains unchanged only if both T and V change proportionally, or if neither changes.

D. Quadruple: Pressure quadruples if temperature quadruples (at constant V), not merely doubles.

Bottom Line: At constant V: P ∝ T (Gay-Lussac's law). At constant T: P ∝ 1/V (Boyle's law). At constant P: V ∝ T (Charles's law). Always use absolute temperature (Kelvin) in gas law calculations — never Celsius.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which intermolecular force is primarily responsible for the unusually high boiling point of water compared to other molecules of similar size?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Hydrogen bonding

Water molecules form strong hydrogen bonds between the partially positive hydrogen atoms of one molecule and the lone pairs on the highly electronegative oxygen atom of a neighboring molecule. Each water molecule can form up to four hydrogen bonds, creating an extensive network that requires substantial energy to disrupt — hence the unusually high boiling point (100°C) for such a small molecule (MW = 18).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. London dispersion forces: These are the weakest intermolecular forces, arising from temporary dipoles; they are present in all molecules but insufficient to explain water's high boiling point.

B. Dipole-dipole interactions: Water does have permanent dipole-dipole interactions, but hydrogen bonding is a particularly strong type of dipole interaction that dominates water's properties.

D. Covalent bonding: Covalent bonds are intramolecular (within molecules, O-H bonds); boiling involves breaking intermolecular forces, not covalent bonds.

Bottom Line: Hydrogen bonds form when H is bonded to F, O, or N (highly electronegative atoms with lone pairs). Water's unique properties — high boiling point, high specific heat, high surface tension, ice floating — all result from extensive hydrogen bonding.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Two molecules with the same molecular formula but differing in the connectivity (order of bonding) of their atoms are called:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Structural (constitutional) isomers

Structural isomers share the same molecular formula but differ in which atoms are bonded to which — they have different connectivity. For example, butane (CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₃) and isobutane (CH₃CH(CH₃)CH₃) both have the formula C₄H₁₀ but different structural arrangements, leading to different physical and chemical properties.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Enantiomers: These are non-superimposable mirror images with the same connectivity but different spatial arrangement around a chiral center (a type of stereoisomer).

B. Geometric isomers: These differ in arrangement around a rigid structure (double bond or ring) — cis vs. trans — but have the same connectivity.

D. Conformational isomers: These are different spatial arrangements of the same molecule achieved by rotation around single bonds (e.g., chair vs. boat cyclohexane); they have identical connectivity.

Bottom Line: Isomers share the same molecular formula. Structural isomers differ in connectivity. Stereoisomers (geometric and optical) have the same connectivity but differ in spatial arrangement. Enantiomers are particularly important in pharmacology — different enantiomers can have dramatically different biological activities.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 8 days. If you start with 100 g, how much remains after 24 days?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: 12.5 g

After each half-life, exactly half of the remaining radioactive atoms decay. In 24 days with a half-life of 8 days, there are 24/8 = 3 half-lives. Starting with 100 g: after 1 half-life (8 days) → 50 g; after 2 half-lives (16 days) → 25 g; after 3 half-lives (24 days) → 12.5 g. The formula is: remaining = initial × (1/2)n, where n = number of half-lives.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 50 g: This would be the amount after only 1 half-life (8 days), not 3.

B. 25 g: This would be the amount after 2 half-lives (16 days).

D. 6.25 g: This would be the amount after 4 half-lives (32 days).

Bottom Line: Remaining amount = Initial × (½)^(t/t½). Half-life is constant regardless of the starting amount. After 10 half-lives, less than 0.1% of the original material remains. This principle is used in radioactive dating, nuclear medicine dosing, and waste disposal planning.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The heme group found in hemoglobin contains an iron ion coordinated within which heterocyclic ring system?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Porphyrin

The heme group consists of a porphyrin ring — a large planar heterocyclic structure made of four pyrrole rings connected by methine bridges. The iron(II) ion sits at the center of this ring, coordinated by four nitrogen atoms from the pyrrole subunits. In hemoglobin, this Fe²⁺ reversibly binds molecular oxygen for transport from lungs to tissues.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Pyrimidine: Pyrimidine is a six-membered ring with two nitrogen atoms; it forms the basis of nucleotide bases (cytosine, thymine, uracil) but is not the ring system in heme.

C. Imidazole: Imidazole is a five-membered ring with two nitrogens found in the amino acid histidine. The proximal histidine in hemoglobin does coordinate to the heme iron, but heme itself is a porphyrin, not an imidazole.

D. Thiazole: Thiazole contains sulfur and nitrogen in a five-membered ring; it is found in thiamine (vitamin B1), not in heme.

Bottom Line: Porphyrins are tetrapyrrole macrocycles that coordinate metal ions. Heme (Fe-porphyrin) is found in hemoglobin, myoglobin, and cytochromes. Chlorophyll (Mg-porphyrin) is the key photosynthetic pigment. Vitamin B12 (Co-corrin) has a related ring structure.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings is classified as:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Exothermic

An exothermic reaction releases energy (as heat) from the system to the surroundings, resulting in a negative enthalpy change (ΔH < 0). The products have lower energy than the reactants, and the temperature of the surroundings increases. Combustion reactions, neutralization of strong acids and bases, and many oxidation reactions are exothermic.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Endothermic: Endothermic reactions absorb heat from surroundings (ΔH > 0), causing the surroundings to cool. Examples: melting ice, dissolving ammonium nitrate.

C. Isothermal: Isothermal means occurring at constant temperature — it describes process conditions, not whether heat is released or absorbed.

D. Adiabatic: Adiabatic means no heat exchange with surroundings (q = 0) — the system's temperature changes instead. This describes isolation conditions, not the direction of heat flow.

Bottom Line: Exothermic: ΔH < 0 (heat released, products lower energy). Endothermic: ΔH > 0 (heat absorbed, products higher energy). Whether a reaction is spontaneous depends on Gibbs free energy (ΔG = ΔH − TΔS), not enthalpy alone.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following elements has the highest electronegativity?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Fluorine (F)

Electronegativity measures an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond. Fluorine has the highest electronegativity of all elements (3.98 on the Pauling scale) due to its small atomic radius and high effective nuclear charge. Electronegativity generally increases across a period (left to right) and decreases down a group (top to bottom) in the periodic table.

ElementElectronegativity (Pauling)Position
Na0.93Group 1 (alkali metal)
C2.55Period 2, Group 14
O3.44Period 2, Group 16
F3.98Period 2, Group 17

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Sodium: As an alkali metal (far left of periodic table), Na has very low electronegativity — it readily loses its valence electron rather than attracting electrons.

B. Carbon: Carbon has intermediate electronegativity, allowing it to form both polar and nonpolar covalent bonds with various elements.

C. Oxygen: Oxygen is highly electronegative (second only to fluorine among common elements) but still less so than fluorine.

Bottom Line: Electronegativity trend: increases across a period, decreases down a group. Most electronegative: F > O > N > Cl. Noble gases are generally not assigned electronegativity values. Electronegativity differences determine bond polarity.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The esterification reaction between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol produces an ester and:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Water

Esterification is a condensation reaction in which the -OH group from the carboxylic acid combines with the -H from the alcohol's hydroxyl group, releasing water (H₂O). The remaining fragments join via an ester bond (C-O-C=O). This reaction is typically acid-catalyzed and reversible; the reverse process (hydrolysis) breaks the ester back into acid and alcohol.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Carbon dioxide: CO₂ is produced in decarboxylation reactions, not esterification.

C. Hydrogen gas: H₂ is released in reactions involving active metals with acids or in certain reduction processes, not in ester formation.

D. An aldehyde: Aldehydes are produced by oxidation of primary alcohols, not by esterification.

Bottom Line: R-COOH + R'-OH ⇌ R-COOR' + H₂O (acid-catalyzed). Esters are responsible for many fruit fragrances and flavors. Important biological esters include triglycerides (fat storage) and aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid). Fischer esterification uses H₂SO₄ as catalyst.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following vitamins is water-soluble and therefore NOT stored in significant quantities in the body?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Vitamin C

Water-soluble vitamins (C and all B vitamins) dissolve in water and are readily excreted by the kidneys when in excess. Because the body cannot store them in large amounts, they must be consumed regularly in the diet. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in adipose tissue and the liver, and excessive intake can lead to toxicity (hypervitaminosis).

CategoryVitaminsStorageToxicity Risk
Fat-solubleA, D, E, KLiver, adipose tissueHigher (accumulate)
Water-solubleC, B-complexMinimal (excreted)Lower (rarely toxic)

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Vitamin A: Fat-soluble; stored in the liver. Excess can cause hypervitaminosis A (headache, liver damage, birth defects).

B. Vitamin D: Fat-soluble; stored in adipose tissue and liver. Excess causes hypercalcemia.

D. Vitamin K: Fat-soluble; stored in the liver. Essential for blood clotting factor synthesis.

Bottom Line: Fat-soluble = ADEK (stored, toxicity possible). Water-soluble = C and B vitamins (need regular intake, excess excreted). Remember: "All Dogs Eat Kibble" for fat-soluble vitamins.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: The oxidation of a primary alcohol first produces which functional group?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Aldehyde

When a primary alcohol (R-CH₂OH) undergoes mild oxidation (e.g., with PCC — pyridinium chlorochromate), the -CH₂OH group is converted to a -CHO (aldehyde) group. If a stronger oxidizing agent is used (e.g., KMnO₄ or K₂Cr₂O₇), or oxidation continues, the aldehyde is further oxidized to a carboxylic acid (-COOH).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Ketone: Ketones are produced by oxidation of secondary alcohols (R₂CHOH → R₂C=O), not primary alcohols.

C. Carboxylic acid: This is the product of FURTHER oxidation of the aldehyde. It is not the first product from a primary alcohol.

D. Ether: Ethers are formed by dehydration (condensation) of two alcohol molecules, not by oxidation.

Bottom Line: Oxidation series: Primary alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid. Secondary alcohol → ketone (cannot be further oxidized without breaking C-C bonds). Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation because there is no H on the carbon bearing -OH.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Proteins, nucleic acids, and polysaccharides are all examples of biological macromolecules formed by which type of polymerization reaction?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Condensation (dehydration) polymerization

Biological macromolecules are assembled by condensation reactions in which monomers are joined with the release of a small molecule — typically water. Amino acids link via peptide bonds (releasing H₂O), nucleotides link via phosphodiester bonds (releasing H₂O), and monosaccharides link via glycosidic bonds (releasing H₂O). These reactions are catalyzed by specific enzymes and require energy input (usually from ATP or GTP).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Addition polymerization: Used in synthetic chemistry (e.g., polyethylene from ethylene) where monomers add without losing atoms; not the mechanism for biological macromolecules.

C. Free radical polymerization: A type of addition polymerization initiated by free radicals; used for industrial polymers like polystyrene, not biological polymers.

D. Ring-opening polymerization: Involves opening cyclic monomers to form polymers; used for some synthetic polymers (e.g., nylon-6), not standard biological macromolecules.

Bottom Line: Condensation = monomers join, water is released. Hydrolysis = water breaks bonds, monomers released (the reverse). All three major biological polymers (proteins, nucleic acids, polysaccharides) are built and broken down by these complementary reactions.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: An atom of carbon-14 (¹⁴C) differs from carbon-12 (¹²C) in that it has:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: 2 more neutrons

Isotopes of an element have the same number of protons (same atomic number) but different numbers of neutrons (different mass number). Carbon always has 6 protons (Z = 6). ¹²C has 12 - 6 = 6 neutrons, while ¹⁴C has 14 - 6 = 8 neutrons — a difference of 2. The extra neutrons make ¹⁴C unstable (radioactive), and it decays by beta emission with a half-life of ~5,730 years.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 2 more protons: Changing the proton number would change the element entirely (to oxygen, Z = 8); isotopes must have the same number of protons.

C. 2 more electrons: Neutral atoms of both isotopes have 6 electrons. Adding electrons would create an ion, not an isotope.

D. 2 fewer protons: This would change the element to beryllium (Z = 4), not produce a carbon isotope.

Bottom Line: Isotopes differ in neutrons only. Same element = same protons. ¹⁴C is used in radiocarbon dating of organic material up to ~50,000 years old. Its constant decay rate allows archaeologists to determine the age of biological specimens.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids occurs in which cellular compartment?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Mitochondrial matrix

Beta-oxidation is the catabolic pathway that breaks down fatty acid chains two carbons at a time, producing acetyl-CoA, NADH, and FADH₂ in the mitochondrial matrix. Long-chain fatty acids require the carnitine shuttle (carnitine palmitoyltransferase I and II) to cross the inner mitochondrial membrane, as CoA derivatives cannot pass directly. The acetyl-CoA produced enters the citric acid cycle for further oxidation.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Cytoplasm: Fatty acid synthesis (lipogenesis) occurs in the cytoplasm, but β-oxidation (breakdown) occurs in the mitochondrial matrix — spatially separated to allow independent regulation.

C. Smooth ER: The smooth ER is involved in lipid synthesis, detoxification, and steroid production, not fatty acid oxidation.

D. Nucleus: The nucleus contains genetic material and is the site of DNA replication and transcription, not metabolic pathways like β-oxidation.

Bottom Line: Each cycle of β-oxidation removes 2 carbons (as acetyl-CoA) and produces 1 NADH + 1 FADH₂. A 16-carbon fatty acid (palmitate) undergoes 7 cycles, yielding 8 acetyl-CoA, 7 NADH, and 7 FADH₂ — generating ~106 ATP total (after subtracting 2 ATP for activation).

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Many enzymes require a non-protein helper molecule to function. A tightly bound, non-protein organic molecule that assists enzyme catalysis is called a:
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Prosthetic group (cofactor)

Many enzymes require additional non-protein components for catalytic activity. A prosthetic group is an organic molecule (often derived from a vitamin) that is tightly and permanently bound to the enzyme. When the helper molecule is loosely associated and dissociates after the reaction, it is called a coenzyme. Metal ions (Zn²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺) that assist catalysis are called metal cofactors. The complete, catalytically active enzyme (protein + cofactor) is called the holoenzyme.

TermDefinitionExample
ApoenzymeProtein part alone (inactive)—
CofactorNon-protein helper (inorganic)Zn²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺
Prosthetic groupTightly bound organic moleculeHeme, FAD, biotin
CoenzymeLoosely bound organic moleculeNAD⁺, CoA, TPP
HoloenzymeApoenzyme + cofactor (active)—

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Substrate: The substrate is the molecule acted upon by the enzyme (converted to product), not a helper molecule.

C. Product: The product is what the enzyme generates from the substrate; it does not assist in catalysis.

D. Inhibitor: An inhibitor reduces or blocks enzyme activity; it opposes enzyme function rather than assisting it.

Bottom Line: Apoenzyme (protein) + cofactor/prosthetic group = holoenzyme (active). Many B vitamins serve as coenzyme precursors: B1→TPP, B2→FAD/FMN, B3→NAD⁺/NADP⁺, B5→CoA, B6→PLP, B7→biotin, B9→THF, B12→methylcobalamin.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the correct name for the compound FeCl₃?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Iron(III) chloride

Iron is a transition metal that can form ions with different charges. In FeCl₃, each chloride ion carries a −1 charge, and three chloride ions give a total negative charge of −3. To balance this, iron must have a +3 charge. Using Stock notation, we write the Roman numeral III in parentheses: iron(III) chloride.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Iron chloride: This name is ambiguous because it does not specify which oxidation state of iron is present. Iron can form both FeCl₂ and FeCl₃, so the charge must be indicated.

B. Ferric dichloride: While "ferric" correctly refers to Fe³⁺, "dichloride" incorrectly indicates only two chloride ions. The correct older name would be ferric chloride (with three chlorides implied by the formula).

C. Iron(II) chloride: Iron(II) chloride would be FeCl₂, where iron has a +2 charge balanced by only two chloride ions. The formula given has three chlorides.

Key Educational Points:

  • Stock notation uses Roman numerals in parentheses to indicate the charge of the metal cation
  • The total positive charge must equal the total negative charge in an ionic compound
  • Older names use suffixes: -ous for the lower charge (ferrous = Fe²⁺) and -ic for the higher charge (ferric = Fe³⁺)

Bottom Line: Transition metals with variable oxidation states require Stock notation — FeCl₃ is iron(III) chloride because iron carries a +3 charge.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the systematic name for HNO₃ when dissolved in water?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Nitric acid

HNO₃ is an oxyacid — an acid containing hydrogen, oxygen, and another element. It is derived from the polyatomic nitrate ion (NO₃−). For oxyacids, the naming rule is: when the polyatomic ion ends in -ate, the acid name uses the suffix -ic acid. Nitrate → nitric acid.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Hydronitric acid: The "hydro-" prefix is only used for binary acids (acids with no oxygen), such as HCl (hydrochloric acid). Since HNO₃ contains oxygen, the hydro- prefix does not apply.

B. Nitrious acid: This is a misspelling, but even "nitrous acid" would be incorrect — nitrous acid is HNO₂, derived from the nitrite ion (NO₂−). The -ite ending corresponds to the -ous acid suffix.

D. Hydrogen nitrate: While this describes the composition correctly, it is not the conventional acid name. This form is used when referring to the pure compound, not the aqueous acid.

Key Educational Points:

  • Oxyacid naming: -ate ion → -ic acid; -ite ion → -ous acid
  • Binary acid naming: hydro- prefix + nonmetal root + -ic acid (e.g., HCl = hydrochloric acid)
  • HNO₃ (nitric acid) and HNO₂ (nitrous acid) form a common pair to remember

Bottom Line: HNO₃ is nitric acid — derived from the nitrate ion, where the -ate suffix converts to -ic acid.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which formula correctly represents calcium phosphate?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Ca₃(PO₄)₂

Calcium forms Ca²⁺ ions, and phosphate is the polyatomic ion PO₄³−. To balance the charges, we need the least common multiple of 2 and 3, which is 6. This requires three calcium ions (3 × +2 = +6) and two phosphate ions (2 × −3 = −6). The formula is therefore Ca₃(PO₄)₂.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. CaPO₄: This gives a total positive charge of +2 (from Ca²⁺) and a total negative charge of −3 (from PO₄³−), which are not balanced. Ionic compounds must be electrically neutral.

B. Ca₂(PO₄)₃: This gives +4 from calcium and −9 from phosphate, which is also not balanced. This formula reverses the correct subscripts.

D. Ca₃PO₄: Without parentheses around the phosphate ion, the subscript 4 would only apply to the last oxygen rather than the entire polyatomic ion. Parentheses are essential when more than one polyatomic ion is needed.

Key Educational Points:

  • Use the criss-cross method: the charge magnitude of one ion becomes the subscript of the other
  • Parentheses are required around polyatomic ions when the subscript is greater than 1
  • Calcium phosphate is a major component of bones and teeth (hydroxyapatite)

Bottom Line: Calcium phosphate is Ca₃(PO₄)₂ — three Ca²⁺ ions balance two PO₄³− ions for electrical neutrality.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the correct name for the compound H₂SO₃(aq)?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Sulfurous acid

H₂SO₃ is an oxyacid derived from the sulfite ion (SO₃²−). When a polyatomic ion ends in -ite, the corresponding acid uses the suffix -ous. Therefore: sulfite → sulfurous acid.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Sulfuric acid: Sulfuric acid is H₂SO₄, derived from the sulfate ion (SO₄²−). The -ate ending gives the -ic acid suffix. H₂SO₄ has one more oxygen than H₂SO₃.

B. Hydrosulfuric acid: The "hydro-" prefix is reserved for binary acids with no oxygen. H₂S(aq) is hydrosulfuric acid. H₂SO₃ contains oxygen, so it cannot use the hydro- prefix.

C. Sulfonic acid: Sulfonic acid (–SO₃H) is an organic functional group where sulfur is bonded to a carbon chain. It is not the name for the inorganic oxyacid H₂SO₃.

Key Educational Points:

  • The -ite/-ous and -ate/-ic pairing is a fundamental naming pattern for oxyacids
  • H₂SO₄ (sulfuric, from sulfate) vs. H₂SO₃ (sulfurous, from sulfite)
  • The hydro- prefix applies only to binary acids (no oxygen present)

Bottom Line: H₂SO₃ is sulfurous acid — the -ite ion (sulfite) gives the -ous acid name.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the correct name for the compound CuSO₄·5H₂O?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate

This compound contains three pieces of information to name: (1) the copper ion's charge, (2) the sulfate polyatomic ion, and (3) the waters of hydration. The sulfate ion is SO₄²− (charge −2), so copper must be Cu²⁺ to balance. The five water molecules attached to the crystal are indicated by the prefix penta- followed by -hydrate.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Copper sulfate pentahydrate: This name omits the Roman numeral. Since copper can be Cu⁺ or Cu²⁺, Stock notation is required to specify which oxidation state is present.

B. Copper(I) sulfate pentahydrate: Copper(I) has a +1 charge, so the formula for copper(I) sulfate would be Cu₂SO₄, not CuSO₄. The 1:1 ratio with sulfate confirms Cu²⁺.

D. Copper(II) sulfate monohydrate: "Mono-" means one water molecule (·1H₂O), but the formula clearly shows ·5H₂O, requiring the prefix "penta-" for five.

Key Educational Points:

  • Hydrates are named using Greek prefixes: mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-, penta-, hexa-
  • The dot (·) in the formula separates the ionic compound from the water of crystallization
  • CuSO₄·5H₂O is a bright blue crystal; the anhydrous form (CuSO₄) is white

Bottom Line: Name hydrates by stating the ionic compound with Stock notation, then the Greek prefix + hydrate for the water molecules.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following is the correct formula for tin(IV) oxide?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: SnO₂

The name "tin(IV) oxide" tells us that tin has a +4 oxidation state (Roman numeral IV) and is combined with oxide ions (O²−). To balance a +4 charge with −2 charges, we need two oxide ions per tin ion: SnO₂.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. SnO: This is tin(II) oxide (stannous oxide), where tin has a +2 charge balanced by one O²−. The (IV) in the name specifies a higher oxidation state.

C. Sn₂O₄: While the ratio of charges would be correct (total +8 and −8), ionic formulas must be written as the simplest whole-number ratio. Sn₂O₄ reduces to SnO₂.

D. Sn₂O: This would require each tin to have a +1 charge, which is not a common oxidation state for tin and does not match tin(IV).

Key Educational Points:

  • The Roman numeral in Stock notation directly tells you the metal's charge
  • Ionic formulas are always written as the lowest whole-number ratio of ions
  • Tin has two common oxidation states: +2 (stannous/tin(II)) and +4 (stannic/tin(IV))

Bottom Line: Tin(IV) oxide is SnO₂ — one Sn⁴⁺ balanced by two O²− ions, written in the simplest ratio.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the correct name for HBr(aq)?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Hydrobromic acid

HBr dissolved in water is a binary acid — an acid composed of hydrogen and one other element, with no oxygen. Binary acids are named with the prefix hydro-, the root of the nonmetal, and the suffix -ic acid. Therefore: hydro- + brom- + -ic acid = hydrobromic acid.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Hydrogen bromide: This is the correct name for HBr as a pure gas, not in aqueous solution. The (aq) designation means it is dissolved in water and behaves as an acid, requiring acid nomenclature.

B. Bromous acid: The suffix -ous would apply to an oxyacid derived from a -ite polyatomic ion (e.g., HBrO₂ is bromous acid). HBr has no oxygen.

C. Bromic acid: The suffix -ic without the hydro- prefix applies to oxyacids derived from -ate ions (e.g., HBrO₃ is bromic acid). HBr is a binary acid, not an oxyacid.

Key Educational Points:

  • Binary acids (no oxygen): hydro- + root + -ic acid
  • The same compound has different names depending on state: HBr(g) is hydrogen bromide; HBr(aq) is hydrobromic acid
  • Common binary acids: HCl (hydrochloric), HBr (hydrobromic), HI (hydroiodic), H₂S (hydrosulfuric), HF (hydrofluoric)

Bottom Line: HBr(aq) is hydrobromic acid — binary acids use the hydro-...-ic acid naming pattern.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which compound is correctly paired with its name?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Mg(OH)₂ — magnesium hydroxide

Compounds containing the hydroxide ion (OH−) are named by stating the metal cation followed by "hydroxide." Magnesium forms Mg²⁺, which requires two OH− ions for charge balance: Mg(OH)₂. The name is simply magnesium hydroxide, with no prefix needed because magnesium has only one common charge.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. NaOH — sodium oxide: NaOH is sodium hydroxide, not sodium oxide. Sodium oxide would be Na₂O. The hydroxide ion (OH−) is a distinct polyatomic ion from the oxide ion (O²−).

C. KOH — potassium hydrogen oxide: KOH is correctly named potassium hydroxide. "Hydrogen oxide" is not used; hydroxide is treated as a single polyatomic ion (OH−), not broken into hydrogen and oxide.

D. Ca(OH)₂ — calcium dihydroxide: Greek prefixes (di-, tri-) are not used in ionic compound nomenclature. The correct name is simply calcium hydroxide. Prefixes are used for covalent molecular compounds (e.g., dinitrogen pentoxide).

Key Educational Points:

  • Hydroxide (OH−) is a polyatomic ion named as one unit
  • Ionic compounds do not use Greek prefixes — the subscript is determined by charge balance
  • Mg(OH)₂ (milk of magnesia) is a common antacid and laxative

Bottom Line: Hydroxides are named as metal + hydroxide, with no Greek prefixes. Mg(OH)₂ is magnesium hydroxide.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: An older chemistry text refers to "cuprous chloride." What is the modern (Stock notation) name and formula?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Copper(I) chloride, CuCl

In the older naming system, the suffix -ous refers to the lower oxidation state, while -ic refers to the higher one. Copper's two common oxidation states are +1 and +2. Therefore, cuprous = Cu⁺ (the lower charge), and cupric = Cu²⁺. Cu⁺ paired with Cl− gives the formula CuCl. In Stock notation: copper(I) chloride.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Copper(II) chloride, CuCl: Both parts are incorrect — cuprous is not Cu²⁺, and CuCl is not the formula for a +2 copper compound.

B. Copper(I) chloride, CuCl₂: While copper(I) is correct for cuprous, the formula CuCl₂ is wrong. Cu⁺ needs only one Cl− to balance the charge.

D. Copper(II) chloride, CuCl₂: This would be "cupric chloride" in the old system. The -ous suffix specifically indicates the lower oxidation state, not the higher one.

Key Educational Points:

  • Classical naming: -ous = lower oxidation state, -ic = higher oxidation state
  • Common pairs: ferrous/ferric (Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺), cuprous/cupric (Cu⁺/Cu²⁺), stannous/stannic (Sn²⁺/Sn⁴⁺)
  • Stock notation (Roman numerals) is now preferred because it is unambiguous

Bottom Line: The -ous suffix indicates the lower charge — cuprous = Cu⁺, so cuprous chloride = copper(I) chloride = CuCl.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Which of the following correctly names the compound (NH₄)₂CO₃?
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Ammonium carbonate

(NH₄)₂CO₃ contains two polyatomic ions: the ammonium cation (NH₄⁺) and the carbonate anion (CO₃²−). Two ammonium ions (+1 each = +2 total) balance one carbonate ion (−2). The compound is named simply by stating the cation followed by the anion: ammonium carbonate.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. Nitrogen hydrogen carbonate: The ammonium ion (NH₄⁺) is a recognized polyatomic ion with its own name. It is not broken down into individual elements when naming compounds.

C. Diammonium tricarbonate: Greek prefixes are not used in ionic nomenclature. Additionally, "tri-" would imply three carbonate ions, but the formula shows only one.

D. Ammonium carbide: Carbide (C⁴− or C₂²−) is a completely different ion from carbonate (CO₃²−). Carbide contains only carbon; carbonate contains carbon and oxygen.

Key Educational Points:

  • Ammonium (NH₄⁺) is the most common polyatomic cation and is named as a unit
  • Carbonate (CO₃²−) should not be confused with carbide (C⁴−) or bicarbonate (HCO₃−)
  • (NH₄)₂CO₃ is historically known as "smelling salts" — it decomposes to release ammonia gas

Bottom Line: (NH₄)₂CO₃ is ammonium carbonate — name both polyatomic ions directly, with no prefixes.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: What is the key structural difference between a sulfonic acid group (–SO₃H) and a carboxylic acid group (–COOH)?
options: The sulfonic acid group contains nitrogen
The carboxylic acid group is always a stronger acid
The sulfonic acid group cannot ionize in water
The sulfonic acid group has sulfur bonded to three oxygen atoms, making it a much stronger acid
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: The sulfonic acid group has sulfur bonded to three oxygen atoms, making it a much stronger acid

In a sulfonic acid (R–SO₃H), sulfur is bonded to three oxygen atoms (two S=O and one S–O–H). The high electronegativity of three oxygen atoms strongly stabilizes the conjugate base (sulfonate, R–SO₃−) through resonance and inductive effects. This makes sulfonic acids very strong acids, comparable in strength to sulfuric acid, and far stronger than carboxylic acids (pKₐ ≈ 4–5).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. The sulfonic acid group contains nitrogen: Sulfonic acid (–SO₃H) contains only sulfur, oxygen, and hydrogen. It is sulfonamides (–SO₂NH₂) that contain nitrogen, which is a derivative, not the acid itself.

B. The carboxylic acid group is always a stronger acid: This is the reverse of reality. Sulfonic acids (pKₐ ≈ −1 to −2) are roughly a million times stronger than typical carboxylic acids (pKₐ ≈ 4–5).

C. The sulfonic acid group cannot ionize in water: Sulfonic acids ionize completely in water — they are strong acids. Their excellent water solubility and complete dissociation make them useful in detergents and pharmaceuticals.

Key Educational Points:

  • Sulfonic acids are strong acids because three electronegative oxygen atoms stabilize the conjugate base
  • The pKₐ of sulfonic acids is typically negative, indicating complete ionization in water
  • Methanesulfonic acid (CH₃SO₃H) and p-toluenesulfonic acid are common laboratory reagents

Bottom Line: Sulfonic acids are far stronger than carboxylic acids because three oxygen atoms on sulfur provide exceptional stabilization of the negative charge in the conjugate base.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Sulfonamide drugs (such as sulfanilamide) were historically significant because they:
options: Worked by inhibiting human cell division
Were the first effective oral antibiotics used to treat bacterial infections
Contained a sulfonic acid group that killed bacteria by lowering pH
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Were the first effective oral antibiotics used to treat bacterial infections

Sulfonamide drugs, introduced in the 1930s, were the first broadly effective antibacterial agents available for systemic use. They contain the sulfonamide group (–SO₂NH₂) and work by mimicking para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), competitively inhibiting the enzyme dihydropteroate synthase in the bacterial folate synthesis pathway. Since humans obtain folate from their diet and lack this enzyme, sulfonamides are selectively toxic to bacteria.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Were the first antiviral medications: Sulfonamides are antibacterial, not antiviral. They target the folate synthesis pathway, which viruses do not possess. Early antiviral drugs like amantadine came decades later.

B. Worked by inhibiting human cell division: Sulfonamides are selectively toxic to bacteria precisely because they target a pathway (folate synthesis) that human cells do not use. Drugs that inhibit human cell division are typically anticancer agents.

D. Contained a sulfonic acid group that killed bacteria by lowering pH: Sulfonamides contain a sulfonamide group (–SO₂NH₂), not a sulfonic acid group (–SO₃H). Their mechanism is competitive enzyme inhibition, not pH alteration.

Key Educational Points:

  • Sulfonamides were the first systemic antibiotics, predating penicillin's widespread clinical use
  • They work by structural mimicry of PABA, blocking bacterial folate biosynthesis
  • The sulfonamide functional group (–SO₂NH₂) is distinct from sulfonic acid (–SO₃H)

Bottom Line: Sulfonamides were the first effective antibacterial drugs, working by competitive inhibition of folate synthesis — a pathway present in bacteria but not humans.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Household detergents often contain sodium alkylbenzenesulfonate. Why are sulfonates effective as surfactants?
options: They are strong oxidizing agents that break down grease
They lower the boiling point of water, allowing grease to evaporate
They react chemically with fats to form soluble glycerol
They have a nonpolar hydrocarbon tail and a highly polar sulfonate head, creating an amphiphilic structure
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: They have a nonpolar hydrocarbon tail and a highly polar sulfonate head, creating an amphiphilic structure

Sodium alkylbenzenesulfonates are amphiphilic molecules — they have a long nonpolar hydrocarbon chain (hydrophobic tail) that dissolves in grease and oil, and a charged sulfonate group (–SO₃−) that is highly soluble in water (hydrophilic head). This dual nature allows them to form micelles that emulsify greasy dirt in water, enabling its removal.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. They are strong oxidizing agents that break down grease: Sulfonates are not oxidizing agents. They physically emulsify grease through micelle formation rather than chemically decomposing it through oxidation.

B. They lower the boiling point of water, allowing grease to evaporate: Surfactants lower the surface tension of water, not its boiling point. Furthermore, grease removal does not occur by evaporation.

C. They react chemically with fats to form soluble glycerol: This describes saponification (base-catalyzed hydrolysis of fats), which is how soap is made, not how detergents work. Detergents emulsify grease without chemically reacting with it.

Key Educational Points:

  • Surfactants reduce water's surface tension, allowing better wetting of surfaces
  • The sulfonate head group (–SO₃−) remains ionized even in hard water, giving detergents an advantage over traditional soaps
  • Micelle formation is a physical process — the nonpolar tails face inward around grease droplets while polar heads face the water

Bottom Line: Sulfonate detergents work because their amphiphilic structure — nonpolar tail plus ionic sulfonate head — lets them emulsify grease in water through micelle formation.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Urea [(NH₂)₂CO] is a carbonic acid derivative with important biological and industrial roles. Which statement about urea is correct?
options: Urea is the primary nitrogenous waste product excreted by humans
Urea contains a sulfur atom in its structure
Urea is a strong acid in aqueous solution
Urea was the first organic compound synthesized but has no modern industrial use
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: Urea is the primary nitrogenous waste product excreted by humans

Urea is synthesized in the liver via the urea cycle, which converts toxic ammonia (from amino acid catabolism) into the much less toxic urea. Urea is then excreted by the kidneys. Its structure consists of a carbonyl group (C=O) flanked by two amine groups (–NH₂), making it a diamide of carbonic acid.

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. Urea contains a sulfur atom: Urea's molecular formula is CH₄N₂O — it contains carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen only. There is no sulfur in its structure.

C. Urea is a strong acid in aqueous solution: Urea is essentially neutral in water (very weakly basic). Its amine groups are poor bases because the adjacent carbonyl group withdraws electron density through resonance. It is used as a protein denaturant, not as an acid or base.

D. Urea was the first organic compound synthesized but has no modern industrial use: While Wöhler's 1828 synthesis of urea from ammonium cyanate was indeed a landmark event, urea is now produced on a massive industrial scale — over 180 million tonnes per year — primarily as a nitrogen-rich fertilizer.

Key Educational Points:

  • Urea is a diamide of carbonic acid: (NH₂)₂CO
  • Wöhler's synthesis disproved vitalism — the idea that organic compounds could only come from living organisms
  • Urea is the world's most widely used nitrogen fertilizer and is also used as a protein denaturant in biochemistry

Bottom Line: Urea is the main nitrogenous waste in humans, a diamide of carbonic acid, and one of the most industrially important organic compounds.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Phosgene (COCl₂) can be viewed as a derivative of carbonic acid. What makes phosgene extremely dangerous?
options: It is a powerful oxidizing agent that ignites spontaneously in air
It is highly toxic because it reacts with nucleophilic groups in lung tissue, causing pulmonary edema
It is radioactive and causes radiation sickness
It is an explosive compound that detonates on contact with water
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: It is highly toxic because it reacts with nucleophilic groups in lung tissue, causing pulmonary edema

Phosgene (COCl₂) is a highly reactive acyl chloride — the dichloride derivative of carbonic acid. When inhaled, it reacts with nucleophilic groups (–NH₂, –OH, –SH) on proteins in the alveolar membranes of the lungs. This cross-linking and acylation damages the lung tissue, leading to fluid accumulation (pulmonary edema) that can be fatal. It was infamously used as a chemical weapon in World War I.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. It is a powerful oxidizing agent that ignites spontaneously in air: Phosgene is not an oxidizing agent and does not spontaneously ignite. Its toxicity comes from its reactivity as an electrophilic acyl chloride, not from redox chemistry.

C. It is radioactive and causes radiation sickness: Phosgene contains only carbon, oxygen, and chlorine — none of which are radioactive in their common isotopes. Its danger is purely chemical.

D. It is an explosive compound that detonates on contact with water: Phosgene does react with water (hydrolysis produces CO₂ and HCl), but this reaction is not explosive. The hydrolysis is relatively slow, which actually increases its danger because inhaled phosgene persists in the lungs.

Key Educational Points:

  • Phosgene is the diacyl chloride of carbonic acid: replacing both –OH groups in (HO)₂CO with –Cl gives COCl₂
  • Its high electrophilicity allows it to acylate biological nucleophiles, causing tissue damage
  • Despite its toxicity, phosgene is used industrially in the production of polycarbonates and polyurethanes

Bottom Line: Phosgene is dangerous because its electrophilic carbonyl reacts with nucleophilic groups in lung proteins, causing lethal pulmonary edema.

chemistry.subject: chemistry
chemistry.question: Guanidine [HN=C(NH₂)₂] is one of the strongest organic bases known. Why is its conjugate acid (guanidinium ion) so stable?
options: The positive charge is stabilized by resonance delocalization over three equivalent nitrogen atoms
It contains an aromatic ring that stabilizes the positive charge
The sulfur atom in guanidine donates electrons to stabilize the cation
Guanidinium is stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding with a carbonyl group
chemistry.explanation:
Correct Answer: The positive charge is stabilized by resonance delocalization over three equivalent nitrogen atoms

When guanidine is protonated, the resulting guanidinium ion [C(NH₂)₃]⁺ has a remarkable symmetry: the positive charge is delocalized equally across three equivalent nitrogen atoms through resonance. This gives a symmetric, Y-shaped structure with three equivalent C–N bonds. The extensive delocalization makes the guanidinium ion exceptionally stable, which is why guanidine is such a strong base (pKₐ of conjugate acid ≈ 13.6).

Why the other choices are wrong:

B. It contains an aromatic ring: Guanidine has no aromatic ring. It is a small, acyclic molecule. The stabilization comes from resonance across the three C–N bonds in a planar, non-cyclic arrangement.

C. The sulfur atom in guanidine donates electrons: Guanidine contains only carbon, nitrogen, and hydrogen — no sulfur. Its formula is HN=C(NH₂)₂.
